THE PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF MICHIGAN ENACT:
1 Sec. 6. (1) "Center program" means a program operated by a
2 district or by an intermediate district for special education
3 pupils from several districts in programs for pupils with autism
4 spectrum disorder, pupils with severe cognitive impairment, pupils
5 with moderate cognitive impairment, pupils with severe multiple
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
3
1 impairments, pupils with hearing impairment, pupils with visual
2 impairment, and pupils with physical impairment or other health
3 impairment. Programs for pupils with emotional impairment housed in
4 buildings that do not serve regular education pupils also qualify.
5 Unless otherwise approved by the department, a center program
6 either serves all constituent districts within an intermediate
7 district or serves several districts with less than 50% of the
8 pupils residing in the operating district. In addition, special
9 education center program pupils placed part-time in noncenter
10 programs to comply with the least restrictive environment
11 provisions of section 1412 of the individuals with disabilities
12 education act, 20 USC 1412, may be considered center program pupils
13 for pupil accounting purposes for the time scheduled in either a
14 center program or a noncenter program.
15 (2) "District and high school graduation rate" means the
16 annual completion and pupil dropout rate that is calculated by the
17 center pursuant to nationally recognized standards.
18 (3) "District and high school graduation report" means a
19 report of the number of pupils, excluding adult education
20 participants, in the district for the immediately preceding school
21 year, adjusted for those pupils who have transferred into or out of
22 the district or high school, who leave high school with a diploma
23 or other credential of equal status.
24 (4) "Membership", except as otherwise provided in this
25 article, means for a district, a public school academy, or an
26 intermediate district the sum of the product of .90 times the
27 number of full-time equated pupils in grades K to 12 actually
28 enrolled and in regular daily attendance in the district, public
29 school academy, or intermediate district on the pupil membership
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
4
1 count day for the current school year, plus the product of .10
2 times the final audited count from the supplemental count day of
3 full-time equated pupils in grades K to 12 actually enrolled and in
4 regular daily attendance in the district, public school academy, or
5 intermediate district for the immediately preceding school year. A
6 district's, public school academy's, or intermediate district's
7 membership is adjusted as provided under section 25e for pupils who
8 enroll after the pupil membership count day in a strict discipline
9 academy operating under sections 1311b to 1311m of the revised
10 school code, MCL 380.1311b to 380.1311m. All pupil counts used in
11 this subsection are as determined by the department and calculated
12 by adding the number of pupils registered for attendance plus
13 pupils received by transfer and minus pupils lost as defined by
14 rules promulgated by the superintendent, and as corrected by a
15 subsequent department audit. The amount of the foundation allowance
16 for a pupil in membership is determined under section 20. In making
17 the calculation of membership, all of the following, as applicable,
18 apply to determining the membership of a district, a public school
19 academy, or an intermediate district:
20 (a) Except as otherwise provided in this subsection, and
21 pursuant to subsection (6), a pupil is counted in membership in the
22 pupil's educating district or districts. Except as otherwise
23 provided in this subsection, an individual pupil must not be
24 counted for more than a total of 1.0 full-time equated membership.
25 (b) If a pupil is educated in a district other than the
26 pupil's district of residence, if the pupil is not being educated
27 as part of a cooperative education program, if the pupil's district
28 of residence does not give the educating district its approval to
29 count the pupil in membership in the educating district, and if the
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
5
1 pupil is not covered by an exception specified in subsection (6) to
2 the requirement that the educating district must have the approval
3 of the pupil's district of residence to count the pupil in
4 membership, the pupil is not counted in membership in any district.
5 (c) A special education pupil educated by the intermediate
6 district is counted in membership in the intermediate district.
7 (d) A pupil placed by a court or state agency in an on-grounds
8 program of a juvenile detention facility, a child caring
9 institution, or a mental health institution, or a pupil funded
10 under section 53a, is counted in membership in the district or
11 intermediate district approved by the department to operate the
12 program.
13 (e) A pupil enrolled in the Michigan Schools for the Deaf and
14 Blind is counted in membership in the pupil's intermediate district
15 of residence.
16 (f) A pupil enrolled in a career and technical education
17 program supported by a millage levied over an area larger than a
18 single district or in an area vocational-technical education
19 program established under section 690 of the revised school code,
20 MCL 380.690, is counted in membership only in the pupil's district
21 of residence.
22 (g) A pupil enrolled in a public school academy is counted in
23 membership in the public school academy.
24 (h) For the purposes of this section and section 6a, for a
25 cyber school, as that term is defined in section 551 of the revised
26 school code, MCL 380.551, that is in compliance with section 553a
27 of the revised school code, MCL 380.553a, a pupil's participation
28 in the cyber school's educational program is considered regular
29 daily attendance, and for a district or public school academy, a
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
6
1 pupil's participation in a virtual course as that term is defined
2 in section 21f is considered regular daily attendance. Beginning
3 July 1, 2021, this subdivision is subject to section 8c. It is the
4 intent of the legislature that the immediately preceding sentence
5 apply retroactively and is effective July 1, 2021. For the purposes
6 of this subdivision, for a pupil enrolled in a cyber school, all of
7 the following apply with regard to the participation requirement as
8 described in this subdivision:
9 (i) Except as otherwise provided in this subdivision, the pupil
10 shall participate in each scheduled course on pupil membership
11 count day or supplemental count day, as applicable. If the pupil is
12 absent on pupil membership count day or supplemental count day, as
13 applicable, the pupil must attend and participate in class during
14 the next 10 consecutive school days if the absence was unexcused,
15 or during the next 30 calendar days if the absence was excused.
16 (ii) For a pupil who is not learning sequentially, 1 or more of
17 the following must be met on pupil membership count day or
18 supplemental count day, as applicable, for each scheduled course to
19 satisfy the participation requirement under this subdivision:
20 (A) The pupil attended a live lesson from the teacher.
21 (B) The pupil logged into a lesson or lesson activity and the
22 login can be documented.
23 (C) The pupil and teacher engaged in a subject-oriented
24 telephone conversation.
25 (D) There is documentation of an email dialogue between the
26 pupil and teacher.
27 (E) There is documentation of activity or work between the
28 learning coach and pupil.
29 (F) An alternate form of attendance as determined and agreed
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
7
1 upon by the cyber school and the pupil membership auditor was met.
2 (iii) For a pupil using sequential learning, the participation
3 requirement under this subdivision is satisfied if either of the
4 following occurs:
5 (A) Except as otherwise provided in this sub-subparagraph, the
6 pupil and the teacher of record or mentor complete a 2-way
7 interaction for 1 course during the week on which pupil membership
8 count day or supplemental count day, as applicable, occurs, and the
9 3 consecutive weeks following that week. However, if a school break
10 is scheduled during any of the weeks described in this sub-
11 subparagraph that is 4 or more days in length or instruction has
12 been canceled districtwide during any of the weeks described in
13 this sub-subparagraph for 3 or more school days, the district is
14 not required to ensure that the pupil and the teacher of record or
15 mentor completed a 2-way interaction for that week. As used in this
16 sub-subparagraph:
17 (I) "2-way interaction" means the communication that occurs
18 between the teacher of record or mentor and pupil, where 1 party
19 initiates communication and a response from the other party follows
20 that communication. Responses as described in this sub-sub-
21 subparagraph must be to the communication initiated by the teacher
22 of record or mentor, and not some other action taken. This
23 interaction may occur through, but is not limited to, means such as
24 email, telephone, instant messaging, or face-to-face conversation.
25 A parent- or legal-guardian-facilitated 2-way interaction is
26 considered a 2-way interaction if the pupil is in any of grades K
27 to 5 and does not yet possess the skills necessary to participate
28 in 2-way interactions unassisted. The interactions described in
29 this sub-sub-subparagraph must relate to a virtual course on the
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
8
1 pupil's schedule and pertain to course content or progress.
2 (II) "Mentor" means a professional employee of the district
3 who monitors the pupil's progress, ensures the pupil has access to
4 needed technology, is available for assistance, and ensures access
5 to the teacher of record. A mentor may also be the teacher of
6 record if the mentor meets the definition of a teacher of record
7 under this sub-subparagraph and the district is the provider for
8 the course.
9 (III) "Teacher of record" means a teacher to whom all of the
10 following apply:
11 (1) The teacher is responsible for providing instruction,
12 determining instructional methods for each pupil, diagnosing
13 learning needs, assessing pupil learning, prescribing intervention
14 strategies and modifying lessons, reporting outcomes, and
15 evaluating the effects of instruction and support strategies. The
16 teacher of record may coordinate the distribution and assignment of
17 the responsibilities described in this sub-sub-sub-subparagraph
18 with other teachers participating in the instructional process for
19 a course.
20 (2) The teacher is certified for the grade level or is working
21 under a valid substitute permit, authorization, or approval issued
22 by the department.
23 (3) The teacher has a personnel identification code provided
24 by the center.
25 (IV) "Week" means a period that starts on Wednesday and ends
26 the following Tuesday.
27 (B) The pupil completes a combination of 1 or more of the
28 following activities for each scheduled course on pupil membership
29 count day or supplemental count day, as applicable:
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
9
1 (I) Documented attendance in a virtual course where
2 synchronous, live instruction occurred with the teacher.
3 (II) Documented completion of a course assignment.
4 (III) Documented completion of a course lesson or lesson
5 activity.
6 (IV) Documented pupil access to an ongoing lesson, which does
7 not include a login.
8 (V) Documented physical attendance on pupil membership count
9 day or supplemental count day, as applicable, in each scheduled
10 course, if the pupil will attend at least 50% of the instructional
11 time for each scheduled course on-site, face-to-face with the
12 teacher of record. As used in this sub-sub-subparagraph, "teacher
13 of record" means that term as defined in subparagraph (iii)(A).
14 (iv) For purposes of subparagraph (iii), each scheduled course
15 currently being attempted by the pupil, rather than every course on
16 the pupil's schedule for the entire term, is considered a part of
17 each scheduled course for the pupil.
18 (i) For a new district or public school academy beginning its
19 operation after December 31, 1994, membership for the first 2 full
20 or partial fiscal years of operation is determined as follows:
21 (i) If operations begin before the pupil membership count day
22 for the fiscal year, membership is the average number of full-time
23 equated pupils in grades K to 12 actually enrolled and in regular
24 daily attendance on the pupil membership count day for the current
25 school year and on the supplemental count day for the current
26 school year, as determined by the department and calculated by
27 adding the number of pupils registered for attendance on the pupil
28 membership count day plus pupils received by transfer and minus
29 pupils lost as defined by rules promulgated by the superintendent,
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
10
1 and as corrected by a subsequent department audit, plus the final
2 audited count from the supplemental count day for the current
3 school year, and dividing that sum by 2.
4 (ii) If operations begin after the pupil membership count day
5 for the fiscal year and not later than the supplemental count day
6 for the fiscal year, membership is the final audited count of the
7 number of full-time equated pupils in grades K to 12 actually
8 enrolled and in regular daily attendance on the supplemental count
9 day for the current school year.
10 (j) If a district is the authorizing body for a public school
11 academy, then, in the first school year in which pupils are counted
12 in membership on the pupil membership count day in the public
13 school academy, the determination of the district's membership
14 excludes from the district's pupil count for the immediately
15 preceding supplemental count day any pupils who are counted in the
16 public school academy on that first pupil membership count day who
17 were also counted in the district on the immediately preceding
18 supplemental count day.
19 (k) For an extended school year program approved by the
20 superintendent, a pupil enrolled, but not scheduled to be in
21 regular daily attendance, on a pupil membership count day, is
22 counted in membership.
23 (l) To be counted in membership, a pupil must meet the minimum
24 age requirement to be eligible to attend school under section 1147
25 of the revised school code, MCL 380.1147, and must be less than 20
26 years of age on September 1 of the school year except as follows:
27 (i) A special education pupil who is enrolled and receiving
28 instruction in a special education program or service approved by
29 the department, who does not have a high school diploma, and who is
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
11
1 less than 26 years of age as of September 1 of the current school
2 year is counted in membership.
3 (ii) A pupil who is determined by the department to meet all of
4 the following may be counted in membership:
5 (A) Is enrolled in a public school academy or an alternative
6 education high school diploma program, that is primarily focused on
7 educating pupils with extreme barriers to education, such as being
8 homeless as that term is defined under 42 USC 11302.
9 (B) Had dropped out of school.
10 (C) Is less than 22 years of age as of September 1 of the
11 current school year.
12 (iii) If a child does not meet the minimum age requirement to be
13 eligible to attend school for that school year under section 1147
14 of the revised school code, MCL 380.1147, but will be 5 years of
15 age not later than December 1 of that school year, the district may
16 count the child in membership for that school year if the parent or
17 legal guardian has notified the district in writing that the parent
18 or legal guardian intends to enroll the child in kindergarten for
19 that school year.
20 (m) An individual who has achieved a high school diploma is
21 not counted in membership unless the individual is enrolled in a
22 state-approved early middle college and at least 1 college course
23 is transcribed for high school credit each membership count during
24 the individual's fifth year. An individual who has achieved a high
25 school equivalency certificate is not counted in membership unless
26 the individual is a student with a disability as that term is
27 defined in R 340.1702 of the Michigan Administrative Code. An
28 individual participating in a job training program funded under
29 former section 107a or a jobs program funded under former section
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
12
1 107b, administered by the department of labor and economic
2 opportunity, or participating in any successor of either of those 2
3 programs, is not counted in membership.
4 (n) If a pupil counted in membership in a public school
5 academy is also educated by a district or intermediate district as
6 part of a cooperative education program, the pupil is counted in
7 membership only in the public school academy unless a written
8 agreement signed by all parties designates the party or parties in
9 which the pupil is counted in membership, and the instructional
10 time scheduled for the pupil in the district or intermediate
11 district is included in the full-time equated membership
12 determination under subdivision (q) and section 101. However, for
13 pupils receiving instruction in both a public school academy and in
14 a district or intermediate district but not as a part of a
15 cooperative education program, the following apply:
16 (i) If the public school academy provides instruction for at
17 least 1/2 of the class hours required under section 101, the public
18 school academy receives as its prorated share of the full-time
19 equated membership for each of those pupils an amount equal to 1
20 times the product of the hours of instruction the public school
21 academy provides divided by the number of hours required under
22 section 101 for full-time equivalency, and the remainder of the
23 full-time membership for each of those pupils is allocated to the
24 district or intermediate district providing the remainder of the
25 hours of instruction.
26 (ii) If the public school academy provides instruction for less
27 than 1/2 of the class hours required under section 101, the
28 district or intermediate district providing the remainder of the
29 hours of instruction receives as its prorated share of the full-
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
13
1 time equated membership for each of those pupils an amount equal to
2 1 times the product of the hours of instruction the district or
3 intermediate district provides divided by the number of hours
4 required under section 101 for full-time equivalency, and the
5 remainder of the full-time membership for each of those pupils is
6 allocated to the public school academy.
7 (o) An individual less than 16 years of age as of September 1
8 of the current school year who is being educated in an alternative
9 education program is not counted in membership if there are also
10 adult education participants being educated in the same program or
11 classroom.
12 (p) The department shall give a uniform interpretation of
13 full-time and part-time memberships.
14 (q) The number of class hours used to calculate full-time
15 equated memberships must be consistent with section 101. In
16 determining full-time equated memberships for pupils who are
17 enrolled in a postsecondary institution or for pupils engaged in an
18 internship or work experience under section 1279h of the revised
19 school code, MCL 380.1279h, a pupil is not considered to be less
20 than a full-time equated pupil solely because of the effect of the
21 pupil's postsecondary enrollment or engagement in the internship or
22 work experience, including necessary travel time, on the number of
23 class hours provided by the district to the pupil.
24 (r) Full-time equated memberships for pupils in kindergarten
25 are determined by dividing the number of instructional hours
26 scheduled and provided per year per kindergarten pupil by the same
27 number used for determining full-time equated memberships for
28 pupils in grades 1 to 12. However, to the extent allowable under
29 federal law, for a district or public school academy that provides
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
14
1 evidence satisfactory to the department that it used federal title
2 I money in the 2 immediately preceding school fiscal years to fund
3 full-time kindergarten, full-time equated memberships for pupils in
4 kindergarten are determined by dividing the number of class hours
5 scheduled and provided per year per kindergarten pupil by a number
6 equal to 1/2 the number used for determining full-time equated
7 memberships for pupils in grades 1 to 12. The change in the
8 counting of full-time equated memberships for pupils in
9 kindergarten that took effect for 2012-2013 is not a mandate.
10 (s) For a district or a public school academy that has pupils
11 enrolled in a grade level that was not offered by the district or
12 public school academy in the immediately preceding school year, the
13 number of pupils enrolled in that grade level to be counted in
14 membership is the average of the number of those pupils enrolled
15 and in regular daily attendance on the pupil membership count day
16 and the supplemental count day of the current school year.
17 Membership is calculated by adding the number of pupils registered
18 for attendance in that grade level on the pupil membership count
19 day plus pupils received by transfer and minus pupils lost as
20 defined by rules promulgated by the superintendent, and as
21 corrected by subsequent department audit, plus the final audited
22 count from the supplemental count day for the current school year,
23 and dividing that sum by 2.
24 (t) A pupil enrolled in a cooperative education program may be
25 counted in membership in the pupil's district of residence with the
26 written approval of all parties to the cooperative agreement.
27 (u) If, as a result of a disciplinary action, a district
28 determines through the district's alternative or disciplinary
29 education program that the best instructional placement for a pupil
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
15
1 is in the pupil's home or otherwise apart from the general school
2 population, if that placement is authorized in writing by the
3 district superintendent and district alternative or disciplinary
4 education supervisor, and if the district provides appropriate
5 instruction as described in this subdivision to the pupil at the
6 pupil's home or otherwise apart from the general school population,
7 the district may count the pupil in membership on a pro rata basis,
8 with the proration based on the number of hours of instruction the
9 district actually provides to the pupil divided by the number of
10 hours required under section 101 for full-time equivalency. For the
11 purposes of this subdivision, a district is considered to be
12 providing appropriate instruction if all of the following are met:
13 (i) The district provides at least 2 nonconsecutive hours of
14 instruction per week to the pupil at the pupil's home or otherwise
15 apart from the general school population under the supervision of a
16 certificated teacher.
17 (ii) The district provides instructional materials, resources,
18 and supplies that are comparable to those otherwise provided in the
19 district's alternative education program.
20 (iii) Course content is comparable to that in the district's
21 alternative education program.
22 (iv) Credit earned is awarded to the pupil and placed on the
23 pupil's transcript.
24 (v) If a pupil was enrolled in a public school academy on the
25 pupil membership count day, if the public school academy's contract
26 with its authorizing body is revoked or the public school academy
27 otherwise ceases to operate, and if the pupil enrolls in a district
28 within 45 days after the pupil membership count day, the department
29 shall adjust the district's pupil count for the pupil membership
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
16
1 count day to include the pupil in the count.
2 (w) For a public school academy that has been in operation for
3 at least 2 years and that suspended operations for at least 1
4 semester and is resuming operations, membership is the sum of the
5 product of .90 times the number of full-time equated pupils in
6 grades K to 12 actually enrolled and in regular daily attendance on
7 the first pupil membership count day or supplemental count day,
8 whichever is first, occurring after operations resume, plus the
9 product of .10 times the final audited count from the most recent
10 pupil membership count day or supplemental count day that occurred
11 before suspending operations, as determined by the superintendent.
12 (x) If a district's membership for a particular fiscal year,
13 as otherwise calculated under this subsection, would be less than
14 1,550 pupils, the district has 4.5 or fewer pupils per square mile,
15 as determined by the department, and the district does not receive
16 funding under section 22d(2), the district's membership is
17 considered to be the membership figure calculated under this
18 subdivision. If a district educates and counts in its membership
19 pupils in grades 9 to 12 who reside in a contiguous district that
20 does not operate grades 9 to 12 and if 1 or both of the affected
21 districts request the department to use the determination allowed
22 under this sentence, the department shall include the square
23 mileage of both districts in determining the number of pupils per
24 square mile for each of the districts for the purposes of this
25 subdivision. If a district has established a community engagement
26 advisory committee in partnership with the department of treasury,
27 is required to submit a deficit elimination plan or an enhanced
28 deficit elimination plan under section 1220 of the revised school
29 code, MCL 380.1220, and is located in a city with a population
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
17
1 between 9,000 and 11,000, as determined by the department, that is
2 in a county with a population between 150,000 and 160,000, as
3 determined by the department, the district's membership is
4 considered to be the The membership figure calculated under this
5 subdivision. The membership figure calculated under this
6 subdivision for a district is the greater of the following:
7 (i) The average of the district's membership for the 3-fiscal-
8 year period ending with that fiscal year, calculated by adding the
9 district's actual membership for each of those 3 fiscal years, as
10 otherwise calculated under this subsection, and dividing the sum of
11 those 3 membership figures by 3.
12 (ii) The district's actual membership for that fiscal year as
13 otherwise calculated under this subsection.
14 (y) Full-time equated memberships for special education pupils
15 who are not enrolled in kindergarten but are enrolled in a
16 classroom program under R 340.1754 of the Michigan Administrative
17 Code are determined by dividing the number of class hours scheduled
18 and provided per year by 450. Full-time equated memberships for
19 special education pupils who are not enrolled in kindergarten but
20 are receiving early childhood special education services under R
21 340.1755 or R 340.1862 of the Michigan Administrative Code are
22 determined by dividing the number of hours of service scheduled and
23 provided per year per pupil by 180.
24 (z) A pupil of a district that begins its school year after
25 Labor Day who is enrolled in an intermediate district program that
26 begins before Labor Day is not considered to be less than a full-
27 time pupil solely due to instructional time scheduled but not
28 attended by the pupil before Labor Day.
29 (aa) For the first year in which a pupil is counted in
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
18
1 membership on the pupil membership count day in a middle college
2 program, the membership is the average of the full-time equated
3 membership on the pupil membership count day and on the
4 supplemental count day for the current school year, as determined
5 by the department. If a pupil described in this subdivision was
6 counted in membership by the operating district on the immediately
7 preceding supplemental count day, the pupil is excluded from the
8 district's immediately preceding supplemental count for the
9 purposes of determining the district's membership.

21 than the fifth Wednesday after the pupil membership count day of
22 the immediately preceding fiscal year.

25 of the identified student percentage multiplied by the total number
26 of pupils in the district, as reported to the center in the form

CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

1 building level. This sub-subparagraph applies only to an eligible
2 district for the fiscal year immediately following the first fiscal
3 year in which it is in the community eligibility program. As used
4 in this sub-subparagraph, "identified student percentage" means the
5 quotient of the number of pupils in an eligible district who are
6 determined to be economically disadvantaged, as reported to the
7 center in a form and manner prescribed by the center not later than
8 the fifth Wednesday after the pupil membership count day in the
9 fiscal year preceding the first fiscal year in which the eligible
10 district is in the community eligibility program, divided by the
11 total number of pupils counted in an eligible district on the pupil
12 membership count day in the fiscal year preceding the first fiscal
13 year in which the eligible district is in the community eligibility
14 program.
15 (C) If the district began operations as a district after the
16 pupil membership count day of the current fiscal year, the number
17 of membership pupils in the district who are determined to be
18 economically disadvantaged, as reported to the center in the form
19 and manner prescribed by the center not later than the fifth
20 Wednesday after the supplemental count day of the current fiscal
21 year.
22 (D) If, for a particular fiscal year, the number of membership
23 pupils in a district who are determined under sub-subparagraph (A)
24 to be economically disadvantaged or to be eligible based on the
25 identified student percentage varies by more than 20 percentage
26 points from the number of those pupils in the district as
27 calculated under sub-subparagraph (A) for the immediately preceding
28 fiscal year caused by an egregious reporting error by the district,

1 subparagraph (A) instead be made using the number of membership

6 year.
7 (ii) Each district or public school academy must be assigned an
8 opportunity index score each fiscal year, the value of which is the
9 quotient of the number of economically disadvantaged pupils as
10 determined under this subdivision for the district or public school
11 academy and the total number of pupils in the district or public
12 school academy in the immediately preceding fiscal year, multiplied
13 by 100 and rounded up to the nearest whole number. Each district or
14 public school academy must be assigned an opportunity index band as
15 follows:

18 receive an additional weighted pupil membership of at least 0.1994
19 and not more than 0.2051 for each economically disadvantaged pupil.

23 (G) As used in this subparagraph, "band adjustment factor"
24 means an amount equal to the difference between the lowest and
25 highest reimbursement bounds for each band, divided by the number
26 of possible opportunity index scores in that band.
27 (c) To assist the legislature in determining necessary funding
28 levels to support foundation allowance payments for weighted pupil
29 membership calculated in this subsection, each revenue estimating
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
48
1 conference conducted under section 367b of the management and
2 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1367b, must include estimated pupil
3 counts necessary to determine a weighted pupil membership under
4 this subsection for the current and subsequent 2 fiscal years.
5 (d) As used in this subsection:
6 (i) "Economically disadvantaged" means a pupil who has been
7 determined eligible for free or reduced-price meals as determined
8 under the Richard B. Russell national school lunch act, 42 USC 1751
9 to 1769j; who is in a household receiving supplemental nutrition
10 assistance program or temporary assistance for needy families
11 assistance; or who is homeless, migrant, or in foster care, as
12 reported to the center.
13 (ii) "English language learner" means limited English
14 proficient pupils who speak a language other than English as their
15 primary language and have difficulty speaking, reading, writing, or
16 understanding English as reported to the center.

11 (2) The appropriations under this section are allocated as
12 provided in this article. Money appropriated under this section
13 from the general fund must be expended to fund the purposes of this
14 article before the expenditure of money appropriated under this
15 section from the state school aid fund.

17 not expended by the end of the fiscal year are transferred to the
18 school aid stabilization fund created under section 11a.
19 Sec. 11a. (1) The school aid stabilization fund is created as
20 a separate account within the state school aid fund.
21 (2) The state treasurer may receive money or other assets from
22 any source for deposit into the school aid stabilization fund. The
23 state treasurer shall deposit into the school aid stabilization
24 fund all of the following:
25 (a) Unexpended and unencumbered state school aid fund revenue
26 for a fiscal year that remains in the state school aid fund as of
27 the bookclosing for that fiscal year.
28 (b) Money statutorily dedicated to the school aid
29 stabilization fund.
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
51
1 (c) Money appropriated to the school aid stabilization fund.
2 (3) Money available in the school aid stabilization fund may
3 not be expended without a specific appropriation from the school
4 aid stabilization fund. Money in the school aid stabilization fund
5 must be expended only for purposes for which state school aid fund
6 money may be expended.
7 (4) The state treasurer shall direct the investment of the
8 school aid stabilization fund. The state treasurer shall credit to
9 the school aid stabilization fund interest and earnings from fund
10 investments.
11 (5) Money in the school aid stabilization fund at the close of
12 a fiscal year remains in the school aid stabilization fund and does
13 not lapse to the unreserved school aid fund balance or the general
14 fund.
15 (6) If the maximum amount appropriated under section 11 from
16 the state school aid fund for a fiscal year exceeds the amount
17 available for expenditure from the state school aid fund for that
18 fiscal year, there is appropriated from the school aid
19 stabilization fund to the state school aid fund an amount equal to
20 the projected shortfall as determined by the department of
21 treasury, but not to exceed available money in the school aid
22 stabilization fund. If the money in the school aid stabilization
23 fund is insufficient to fully fund an amount equal to the projected
24 shortfall, the state budget director shall notify the legislature
25 as required under section 296(2) and state payments in an amount
26 equal to the remainder of the projected shortfall must be prorated
27 in the manner provided under section 296(3).
28 (7) For 2025-2026 2026-2027, in addition to the appropriations
29 in section 11, there is appropriated from the school aid
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
52
1 stabilization fund to the state school aid fund the amount
2 necessary to fully fund the allocations under this article.

12 Sec. 19. (1) A district or intermediate district shall comply
13 with all applicable reporting requirements specified in state and
14 federal law. Data provided to the center, in a form and manner
15 prescribed by the center, must be aggregated and disaggregated as
16 required by state and federal law. In addition, a district or
17 intermediate district shall cooperate with all measures taken by
18 the center to establish and maintain a statewide P-20 longitudinal
19 data system.
20 (2) Each district shall furnish to the center not later than 5
21 weeks after the pupil membership count day and by the last business
22 day in June of the school fiscal year ending in the fiscal year, in
23 a manner prescribed by the center, the information necessary for
24 the preparation of the district and high school graduation report,
25 information regarding completion of early middle college
26 credentials obtained and postsecondary credits obtained in any
27 college acceleration program, and information necessary for the
28 preparation of the state and federal accountability reports. This
29 information must meet requirements established in the pupil
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
73
1 auditing manual approved and published by the department. The
2 center shall calculate an annual graduation and pupil dropout rate
3 for each high school, each district, and this state, in compliance
4 with nationally recognized standards for these calculations. The
5 center shall report all graduation and dropout rates to the senate
6 and house education committees and appropriations committees, the
7 state budget director, and the department not later than 30 days
8 after the publication of the list described in subsection (5).
9 Before reporting these graduation and dropout rates, the department
10 shall allow a school or district to appeal the calculations. The
11 department shall consider and act upon the appeal within 30 days
12 after it is submitted and shall not report these graduation and
13 dropout rates until after all appeals have been considered and
14 decided.
15 (3) By the first business day in December and by the last
16 business day in June of each year, and within 30 days of any change
17 in employment or assignment status related to a criminal act, a
18 district shall furnish to the center, in a manner prescribed by the
19 center, information related to educational personnel and personnel
20 vacancies as necessary for reporting required by state and federal
21 law. For the purposes of this subsection, the center shall only
22 require districts and intermediate districts to report information
23 that is not already available from the office of retirement
24 services in the department of technology, management, and budget,
25 including, but not limited to, vacancy start and end dates, reasons
26 for the vacancy, and vacancy termination.
27 (4) If a district or intermediate district fails to meet the
28 requirements of this section, the department shall withhold 5% of
29 the total funds for which the district or intermediate district
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
74
1 qualifies under this article until the district or intermediate
2 district complies with this section. If the district or
3 intermediate district does not comply with this section by the end
4 of the fiscal year, the department shall place the amount withheld
5 in an escrow account until the district or intermediate district
6 complies with this section.

8 accountability designations as required by the no child left behind

26 foundation allowance for the immediately preceding fiscal year, the

29 current fiscal year.

1 (b) For a district that had a foundation allowance for the

1 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1367b).

16 (11) To assist the legislature in determining the target
17 foundation allowance for the subsequent fiscal year, each revenue
18 estimating conference conducted under section 367b of the
19 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1367b, must
20 calculate a pupil membership factor, a revenue adjustment factor,
21 and an index as follows:
22 (a) The pupil membership factor is computed by dividing the
23 estimated membership in the school year ending in the current
24 fiscal year, excluding intermediate district membership, by the
25 estimated membership for the school year ending in the subsequent
26 fiscal year, excluding intermediate district membership. If a
27 consensus membership factor is not determined at the revenue
28 estimating conference, the principals of the revenue estimating
29 conference shall report their estimates to the house and senate
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
81
1 subcommittees responsible for school aid appropriations not later
2 than 7 days after the conclusion of the revenue conference.
3 (b) The revenue adjustment factor is computed by dividing the
4 sum of the estimated total state school aid fund revenue for the
5 subsequent fiscal year plus the estimated total state school aid
6 fund revenue for the current fiscal year, adjusted for any change
7 in the rate or base of a tax the proceeds of which are deposited in
8 that fund and excluding money transferred into that fund from the
9 countercyclical budget and economic stabilization fund under the
10 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101 to 18.1594, by
11 the sum of the estimated total school aid fund revenue for the
12 current fiscal year plus the estimated total state school aid fund
13 revenue for the immediately preceding fiscal year, adjusted for any
14 change in the rate or base of a tax the proceeds of which are
15 deposited in that fund. If a consensus revenue factor is not
16 determined at the revenue estimating conference, the principals of
17 the revenue estimating conference shall report their estimates to
18 the house and senate subcommittees responsible for school aid
19 appropriations not later than 7 days after the conclusion of the
20 revenue conference.
21 (c) The index is calculated by multiplying the pupil
22 membership factor by the revenue adjustment factor. If a consensus
23 index is not determined at the revenue estimating conference, the
24 principals of the revenue estimating conference shall report their
25 estimates to the house and senate subcommittees responsible for
26 state school aid appropriations not later than 7 days after the
27 conclusion of the revenue conference.

5 Sec. 21f. (1) A primary district shall enroll an eligible
6 pupil in virtual courses in accordance with the provisions of this
7 section. A primary district shall not offer a virtual course to an
8 eligible pupil unless the virtual course is published in the
9 primary district's catalog of board-approved courses or in the
10 statewide catalog of virtual courses maintained by the Michigan
11 Virtual University pursuant to section 98. The primary district
12 shall also provide on its publicly accessible website a link to the
13 statewide catalog of virtual courses maintained by the Michigan
14 Virtual University. Unless the pupil is at least age 18 or is an
15 emancipated minor, a pupil must not be enrolled in a course that
16 meets virtually for more than 6 days in a school year without the
17 consent of the pupil's parent or legal guardian.
18 (2) Subject to subsection (3), a primary district shall enroll
19 an eligible pupil in up to 2 virtual courses as requested by the
20 pupil during an academic term, semester, or trimester.
21 (3) A pupil may be enrolled in more than 2 virtual courses in
22 a specific academic term, semester, or trimester if both of the
23 following conditions are met:
24 (a) The primary district has determined that it is in the best
25 interest of the pupil.
26 (b) The pupil agrees with the recommendation of the primary
27 district.
28 (4) If the number of applicants eligible for acceptance in a
29 virtual course does not exceed the capacity of the provider to
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
88
1 provide the virtual course, the provider shall accept for
2 enrollment all of the applicants eligible for acceptance. If the
3 number of applicants exceeds the provider's capacity to provide the
4 virtual course, the provider shall use a random draw system,
5 subject to the need to abide by state and federal
6 antidiscrimination laws and court orders. A primary district that
7 is also a provider shall determine whether or not it has the
8 capacity to accept applications for enrollment from nonresident
9 applicants in virtual courses and may use that limit as the reason
10 for refusal to enroll a nonresident applicant.
11 (5) A primary district may not establish additional
12 requirements beyond those specified in this subsection that would
13 prohibit a pupil from taking a virtual course. A pupil's primary
14 district may deny the pupil enrollment in a virtual course if any
15 of the following apply, as determined by the district:
16 (a) The pupil is enrolled in any of grades K to 5.
17 (b) The pupil has previously gained the credits that would be
18 provided from the completion of the virtual course.
19 (c) The virtual course is not capable of generating academic
20 credit.
21 (d) The virtual course is inconsistent with the remaining
22 graduation requirements or career interests of the pupil.
23 (e) The pupil has not completed the prerequisite coursework
24 for the requested virtual course or has not demonstrated
25 proficiency in the prerequisite course content.
26 (f) The pupil has failed a previous virtual course in the same
27 subject during the 2 most recent academic years.
28 (g) The virtual course is of insufficient quality or rigor. A
29 primary district that denies a pupil enrollment request for this
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
89
1 reason shall enroll the pupil in a virtual course in the same or a
2 similar subject that the primary district determines is of
3 acceptable rigor and quality.
4 (h) The cost of the virtual course exceeds the amount
5 identified in subsection (10), unless the pupil or the pupil's
6 parent or legal guardian agrees to pay the cost that exceeds this
7 amount.
8 (i) The request for a virtual course enrollment did not occur
9 within the same timelines established by the primary district for
10 enrollment and schedule changes for regular courses.
11 (j) The request for a virtual course enrollment was not made
12 in the academic term, semester, trimester, or summer preceding the
13 enrollment. This subdivision does not apply to a request made by a
14 pupil who is newly enrolled in the primary district.
15 (6) If a pupil is denied enrollment in a virtual course by the
16 pupil's primary district, the primary district shall provide
17 written notification to the pupil of the denial, the reason or
18 reasons for the denial under subsection (5), and a description of
19 the appeal process. The pupil may appeal the denial by submitting a
20 letter to the superintendent of the intermediate district in which
21 the pupil's primary district is located. The letter of appeal must
22 include the reason provided by the primary district for not
23 enrolling the pupil and the reason why the pupil is claiming that
24 the enrollment should be approved. The intermediate district
25 superintendent or designee shall respond to the appeal within 5
26 days after it is received. If the intermediate district
27 superintendent or designee determines that the denial of enrollment
28 does not meet 1 or more of the reasons specified in subsection (5),
29 the primary district shall enroll the pupil in the virtual course.
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
90
1 (7) To provide a virtual course to an eligible pupil under
2 this section, a provider must do all of the following:
3 (a) Ensure that the virtual course has been published in the
4 pupil's primary district's catalog of board-approved courses or
5 published in the statewide catalog of virtual courses maintained by
6 the Michigan Virtual University.
7 (b) Assign to each pupil a teacher of record and provide the
8 primary district with the personnel identification code assigned by
9 the center for the teacher of record. If the provider is a
10 community college, the virtual course must be taught by an
11 instructor employed by or contracted through the providing
12 community college.
13 (c) Offer the virtual course on an open entry and exit method,
14 or aligned to a semester, trimester, or accelerated academic term
15 format.
16 (d) If the virtual course is offered to eligible pupils in
17 more than 1 district, the following additional requirements must
18 also be met:
19 (i) Provide the Michigan Virtual University with a course
20 syllabus that meets the definition under subsection (14)(g) in a
21 form and manner prescribed by the Michigan Virtual University for
22 inclusion in a statewide catalog of virtual courses.
23 (ii) Not later than October 1 of each fiscal year, provide the
24 Michigan Virtual University with an aggregated count of enrollments
25 for each virtual course the provider delivered to pupils under this
26 section during the immediately preceding school year, and the
27 number of enrollments in which the pupil earned 60% or more of the
28 total course points for each virtual course.
29 (8) To provide a virtual course under this section, a
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
91
1 community college shall ensure that each virtual course it provides
2 under this section generates postsecondary credit.
3 (9) For any virtual course a pupil enrolls in under this
4 section, the pupil's primary district must assign to the pupil a
5 mentor and shall supply the provider with the mentor's contact
6 information.
7 (10) For a pupil enrolled in 1 or more virtual courses, the
8 primary district shall use foundation allowance or per-pupil funds
9 calculated under section 20 to pay for the expenses associated with
10 the virtual course or courses. A primary district is not required
11 to pay toward the cost of a virtual course an amount that exceeds
12 6.67% of the target foundation allowance for the current fiscal
13 year as calculated under section 20.
14 (11) A virtual learning pupil has the same rights and access
15 to technology in the pupil's primary district's school facilities
16 as all other pupils enrolled in the pupil's primary district. The
17 department shall establish standards for hardware, software, and
18 internet access for pupils who are enrolled in more than 2 virtual
19 courses under this section in an academic term, semester, or
20 trimester taken at a location other than a school facility.
21 (12) If a pupil successfully completes a virtual course, as
22 determined by the pupil's primary district, the pupil's primary
23 district shall grant appropriate academic credit for completion of
24 the course and shall count that credit toward completion of
25 graduation and subject area requirements. A pupil's school record
26 and transcript must identify the virtual course title as it appears
27 in the virtual course syllabus.
28 (13) The enrollment of a pupil in 1 or more virtual courses
29 must not result in a pupil being counted as more than 1.0 full-time
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
92
1 equivalent pupils under this article. The minimum requirements to
2 count the pupil in membership are those established by the pupil
3 accounting manual as it was in effect for the 2015-2016 school year
4 or as subsequently amended by the department if the department
5 notifies the legislature about the proposed amendment at least 60
6 days before the amendment becomes effective.
7 (14) Subject to the requirements of this subsection, a
8 district may provide virtual instruction under this subsection for
9 not more than 6 days in a school year. If a district plans to
10 provide instruction under this subsection to pupils for more than 6
11 days during a school year, the district's plan must be approved by
12 the board of the district, and the district must provide notice of
13 the plan to impacted pupils and their parents or legal guardians
14 before enactment of the plan. Instruction may be provided
15 synchronously or asynchronously, as determined by the department.
16 Days of instruction under this subsection may be used only for the
17 following purposes, as defined by the department:
18 (a) A maximum of 3 days may be designated as testing days,
19 during which students are released from regular classroom
20 instruction to participate in state or federally mandated
21 assessments. Days of virtual instruction provided under this
22 subdivision are limited to buildings and grade levels impacted by
23 assessments.
24 (b) A maximum of 3 days of virtual instruction may be provided
25 due to conditions not within control of the district, including,
26 but not limited to, closures due to snow, ice, or other extreme
27 weather events or unforeseen emergencies. Days of virtual
28 instruction under this subdivision may be used only after a
29 district has exhausted all emergency closure days permitted under
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

1 section 101.
2 (15) The department shall promulgate rules for the conditions

5 (16) (14) As used in this section:
6 (a) "Instructor" means an individual who is employed by or
7 contracted through a community college.
8 (b) "Mentor" means a professional employee of the primary
9 district who monitors the pupil's progress, ensures the pupil has
10 access to needed technology, is available for assistance, and
11 ensures access to the teacher of record. A mentor may also serve as
12 the teacher of record if the primary district is the provider for
13 the virtual course and the mentor meets the requirements under
14 subdivision (e).
15 (c) "Primary district" means the district that enrolls the
16 pupil and reports the pupil for pupil membership purposes.
17 (d) "Provider" means the district, intermediate district,
18 community college, or other third-party vendor that the primary
19 district pays to provide the virtual course or the Michigan Virtual
20 University if it is providing the virtual course.
21 (e) "Teacher of record" means a teacher who meets all of the
22 following:
23 (i) Is appropriately placed under a valid Michigan teaching
24 certificate or a teaching permit, authorization, or approval issued
25 by the department. As used in this subparagraph, "appropriately
26 placed" means holding a valid Michigan educator credential with the
27 required grade range and discipline or subject area for the
28 assignment, as defined by the superintendent of public instruction.
29 (ii) Is responsible for providing instruction, determining
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
94
1 instructional methods for each pupil, diagnosing learning needs,
2 assessing pupil learning, prescribing intervention strategies and
3 modifying lessons, reporting outcomes, and evaluating the effects
4 of instruction and support strategies.
5 (iii) Has a personnel identification code provided by the
6 center.
7 (iv) If the provider is a community college, is an instructor
8 employed by or contracted through the providing community college.
9 (f) "Virtual course" means a course of study that is capable
10 of generating a credit or a grade and that is provided in an
11 interactive learning environment where any portion of the
12 curriculum is delivered using the internet and in which pupils may
13 be separated from their instructor or teacher of record by time or
14 location, or both.
15 (g) "Virtual course syllabus" means a document that includes
16 all of the following:
17 (i) An alignment document detailing how the course meets
18 applicable state standards or, if the state does not have state
19 standards, nationally recognized standards.
20 (ii) The virtual course content outline.
21 (iii) The virtual course required assessments.
22 (iv) The virtual course prerequisites.
23 (v) Expectations for actual instructor or teacher of record
24 contact time with the virtual learning pupil and other
25 communications between a pupil and the instructor or teacher of
26 record.
27 (vi) Academic support available to the virtual learning pupil.
28 (vii) The virtual course learning outcomes and objectives.
29 (viii) The name of the institution or organization providing the
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
95
1 virtual content.
2 (ix) The name of the institution or organization providing the
3 instructor or teacher of record.
4 (x) The course titles assigned by the provider and the course
5 titles and course codes from the National Center for Education
6 Statistics (NCES) school codes for the exchange of data (SCED).
7 (xi) The number of eligible pupils that will be accepted by the
8 provider in the virtual course. A primary district that is also the
9 provider may limit the enrollment to those pupils enrolled in the
10 primary district.
11 (xii) The results of the virtual course quality review using
12 the guidelines and model review process published by the Michigan
13 Virtual University.
14 (h) "Virtual learning pupil" means a pupil enrolled in 1 or
15 more virtual courses.
16 Sec. 21h. (1) From the state school aid pupil support reserve
17 fund money appropriated in section 11, there is allocated
18 $6,137,400.00 for 2025-2026 2026-2027 for assisting districts
19 assigned by the superintendent to participate in a partnership
20 district agreement to improve student achievement and district
21 financial stability. It is the intent of the legislature that the
22 appropriation in this section will be funded with state school aid
23 pupil support reserve fund money through 2027-2028. The
24 superintendent shall identify any conditions that may be
25 contributing to low academic performance within a district being
26 considered for assignment to a partnership district agreement. The
27 purpose of the partnership district agreement is to identify
28 district needs, develop intervention plans, and partner with
29 public, private, and nonprofit organizations to coordinate
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
96
1 resources and improve student achievement. Assignment of a district
2 to a partnership district agreement is made by the superintendent.
3 (2) A district described in subsection (1) is eligible for
4 funding under this section if the district includes at least 1
5 school that has been identified as low performing under the
6 approved federal accountability system. A district described in
7 this subsection must do all of the following to be eligible for
8 funding under this section:
9 (a) Within 90 days of assignment to the partnership district
10 agreement described in this section, complete a comprehensive needs
11 assessment or evaluation in collaboration with an intermediate
12 district, community members, education organizations, and
13 postsecondary institutions, as applicable, that is approved by the
14 superintendent. The comprehensive needs assessment or evaluation
15 must include at least all of the following:
16 (i) A review of the district's implementation and utilization
17 of a multi-tiered system of supports to ensure that it is used to
18 appropriately inform instruction.
19 (ii) A review of the district and school building leadership
20 and educator capacity to substantially improve student outcomes.
21 (iii) A review of classroom, instructional, and operational
22 practices and curriculum to ensure alignment with research-based
23 instructional practices and state curriculum standards.
24 (b) Develop a district continuous improvement plan that has
25 been approved by the superintendent and that addresses the needs
26 identified in the comprehensive needs assessment or evaluation
27 completed under subdivision (a). The district continuous
28 improvement plan must include at least all of the following:
29 (i) Specific actions that will be taken by the district and
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
97
1 each of its partners to improve student achievement.
2 (ii) Specific measurable benchmarks that will be met within 18
3 months to improve student achievement and identification of
4 expected student achievement outcomes to be attained within 3 years
5 after assignment to the partnership.
6 (iii) Measurable benchmarks that put pupils on track to meet or
7 exceed grade level proficiency, increase high school graduation
8 rates, reduce class sizes in grades K to 3, and improve attendance
9 rates.
10 (c) Provide access to training for district leadership,
11 including, but not limited to, the superintendent or chief
12 administrator and school board or board of directors members, on
13 areas of education fiscal and policy matters. The department may
14 require training for district leadership and all board members
15 under this subdivision at a rate and frequency needed to support
16 measurable academic outcomes for the district.
17 (3) Upon approval of the district continuous improvement plan
18 developed under subsection (2), the department shall assign a team
19 of individuals with expertise in comprehensive school and district
20 reform to partner with the district, the intermediate district,
21 community organizations, education organizations, and postsecondary
22 institutions identified in the academic and financial operating or
23 intervention district continuous improvement plan to review the
24 district's use of existing financial resources to ensure that those
25 resources are being used as efficiently and effectively as possible
26 to improve student academic achievement and to ensure district
27 financial stability. The superintendent of public instruction may
28 waive burdensome administrative rules for a partnership district
29 for the duration of the partnership district agreement.

1 (b) Utilizing the infrastructure to put in place commonly

11 reduced cost meal reimbursement rates and CEP eligibility

25 (4) Each district or public school academy must be assigned an
26 opportunity index score each fiscal year, the value of which is the
27 quotient of the number of economically disadvantaged pupils as
28 determined under subsection (3) for the district or public school
29 academy and the total number of pupils in the district or public
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
160
1 school academy in the immediately preceding fiscal year, multiplied
2 by 100 and rounded up to the nearest whole number. Each district or
3 public school academy must be assigned an opportunity index band as
4 follows:
5 (a) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
6 index score greater than or equal to 0 but less than 20 must be
7 assigned to band 1 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
8 (3) at a rate of at least 35.0% and less than 36.0%. The
9 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
10 to the district's opportunity index score minus 1, multiplied by
11 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
12 35.0%.
13 (b) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
14 index score greater than or equal to 20 but less than 44 must be
15 assigned to band 2 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
16 (3) at a rate of at least 36.0% and less than 37.5%. The
17 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
18 to the district's opportunity index score minus 20, multiplied by
19 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
20 36.0%.
21 (c) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
22 index score greater than or equal to 44 but less than 59 must be
23 assigned to band 3 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
24 (3) at a rate of at least 37.5% and less than 39.0%. The
25 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
26 to the district's opportunity index score minus 44, multiplied by
27 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
28 37.5%.
29 (d) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
161
1 index score greater than or equal to 59 but less than 73 must be
2 assigned to band 4 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
3 (3) at a rate of at least 39.0% and less than 42.0%. The
4 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
5 to the district's opportunity index score minus 59, multiplied by
6 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
7 39.0%.
8 (e) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
9 index score greater than or equal to 73 but less than 85 must be
10 assigned to band 5 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
11 (3) at a rate of at least 42.0% and less than 47.0%. The
12 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
13 to the district's opportunity index score minus 73, multiplied by
14 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
15 42.0%.
16 (f) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
17 index score greater than or equal to 85 must be assigned to band 6
18 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection (3) at a rate of
19 47.0%.
20 (g) As used in this subsection, "band adjustment factor" means
21 an amount equal to the difference between the lowest and highest
22 reimbursement bounds for each band, divided by the number of
23 possible opportunity index scores in that band.

6 (24) As used in this section:
7 (a) "At-risk pupil" means a pupil in grades pre-K to 12 for
8 whom the district has documentation that the pupil meets any of the
9 following criteria:
10 (i) The pupil is economically disadvantaged.
11 (ii) The pupil is an English language learner.
12 (iii) The pupil is chronically absent as defined by and reported
13 to the center.
14 (iv) The pupil is a victim of child abuse or neglect.
15 (v) The pupil is a pregnant teenager or teenage parent.
16 (vi) The pupil has a family history of school failure,
17 incarceration, or substance abuse.
18 (vii) The pupil is an immigrant who has immigrated within the
19 immediately preceding 3 years.
20 (viii) The pupil did not complete high school in 4 years and is
21 still continuing in school as identified in the Michigan cohort
22 graduation and dropout report.
23 (ix) For pupils for whom the results of the state summative
24 assessment have been received, is a pupil who did not achieve
25 proficiency on the English language arts, mathematics, science, or
26 social studies content area assessment.
27 (x) Is a pupil who is at risk of not meeting the district's or
28 public school academy's core academic curricular objectives in
29 English language arts or mathematics, as demonstrated on local
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
170
1 assessments.
2 (b) "Economically disadvantaged" means a pupil who has been
3 determined eligible for free or reduced-price meals as determined
4 under the Richard B. Russell national school lunch act, 42 USC 1751
5 to 1769j; who is in a household receiving supplemental nutrition
6 assistance program or temporary assistance for needy families
7 assistance; or who is homeless, migrant, or in foster care, as
8 reported to the center.
9 (c) "English language learner" means limited English
10 proficient pupils who speak a language other than English as their
11 primary language and have difficulty speaking, reading, writing, or
12 understanding English as reported to the center.

1 (i) That the applicant complies with all program components
2 described in subsection (4).
3 (ii) That the applicant meets the requirements of a waiver
4 under subsection (5).
5 (b) Except as otherwise provided in this subdivision, ensure
6 that children participating in an eligible great start readiness
7 program for whom the intermediate district is receiving funds under
8 this section are children who live with families with a household
9 income that is equal to or less than 400% of the federal poverty
10 guidelines. If the intermediate district determines that all
11 eligible children are being served and that there are no children
12 on the waiting list who live with families with a household income
13 that is equal to or less than 400% of the federal poverty
14 guidelines, the intermediate district may then enroll children who
15 live with families with a household income that is greater than
16 400% of the federal poverty guidelines. The enrollment process must
17 consider income and risk factors, such that children determined
18 with higher need are enrolled before children with lesser need. For
19 purposes of this subdivision, all age-eligible children served in
20 foster care or who are experiencing homelessness or who have
21 individualized education programs recommending placement in an
22 inclusive preschool setting are considered to live with families
23 with household income equal to or less than 400% of the federal
24 poverty guidelines regardless of actual family income and are
25 prioritized for enrollment within the lowest quintile. The
26 department of lifelong education, advancement, and potential shall
27 publish the household income thresholds under this subdivision in a
28 clear manner on its website and the great start to quality website.

16 (17) Each grant recipient shall enroll children identified
17 under subsection (8)(b) according to how far the child's household
18 income is below 400% of the federal poverty guidelines by ranking
19 each applicant child's household income from lowest to highest and
20 dividing the applicant children into quintiles based on how far the
21 child's household income is below 400% of the federal poverty
22 guidelines, and then enrolling children in the quintile with the
23 lowest household income before enrolling children in the quintile
24 with the next lowest household income until slots are completely
25 filled. If the grant recipient determines that all eligible
26 children are being served and that there are no children on the
27 waiting list who live with families with a household income that is
28 equal to or less than 400% of the federal poverty guidelines, the
29 grant recipient may then enroll children who live with families
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

1 (19) (22) A recipient of funds under this section shall report
2 to the center in a form and manner prescribed by the center the

10 (20) (23) As used in this section:
11 (a) "Child care center" means that term as defined in section
12 1 of 1973 PA 116, MCL 722.111.
13 (b) "Eligible great start readiness program options" means a
14 program option that operates on a school-day, part-day, or extended
15 schedule length, as determined by the department of lifelong
16 education, advancement, and potential. The department of lifelong
17 education, advancement, and potential must maintain and publish on
18 its website requirements for each eligible schedule length,
19 including the minimum day length, the minimum number of days per
20 week, and the minimum number of weeks per year. These programs may
21 be blended with Head Start programs, if allowable by federal rules
22 and regulations.
23 (c) "Federal poverty guidelines" means the guidelines
24 published annually in the Federal Register by the United States
25 Department of Health and Human Services under its authority to
26 revise the poverty line under 42 USC 9902.
27 (d) "GSRP extended program" means a program that operates for
28 at least the same length of day as a district's first grade program
29 for a minimum of 5 days per week, 36 weeks per year.
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
213
1 (e) "GSRP/Head Start extended blended program" means a program
2 funded under this section and a Head Start program that are
3 combined for an extended program.
4 (f) "GSRP/Head Start school-day blended program" means a part-
5 day program funded under this section and a Head Start program,
6 which are combined for a school-day program.
7 (g) "Licensed child care center" means a child care center
8 that has been issued a license under 1973 PA 116, MCL 722.111 to
9 722.128, to operate a child care center.
10 (h) "Part-day program" means a program that operates at least
11 4 days per week, 30 weeks per year, for at least 3 hours of
12 teacher-child contact time per day but for fewer hours of teacher-
13 child contact time per day than a school-day program.
14 (i) "School-day program" means a program that operates for at
15 least the same length of day as a district's first grade program
16 for a minimum of 4 days per week, 30 weeks per year. A classroom
17 that offers a school-day program must enroll all children for the
18 school day to be considered a school-day program.

5 116, MCL 722.111 to 722.128, and will be a licensed child care
6 organization before the entity provides services for which a child
7 care organization is required to be licensed under 1973 PA 116, MCL
8 722.111 to 722.128.

10 section 1 of 1973 PA 116, MCL 722.111.
11 (c) "Exempt entity" means an entity described in section
12 1(1)(i)(i) to (v) of 1973 PA 116, MCL 722.111.
13 (d) "Licensed child care organization" means a child care
14 organization that has been issued a license under 1973 PA 116, MCL
15 722.111 to 722.128, to operate a child care organization.
16 (e) "School-age" means a child who is eligible to attend a
17 grade of kindergarten or higher, but is less than 13 years of age.
18 A child is considered to be school age on the first day of the
19 school year in which the child is eligible under section 1 of 1973
20 PA 116, MCL 722.111.

4 Sec. 32p. (1) From the state school aid fund money
5 appropriated in section 11, there is allocated an amount not to
6 exceed $30,000,000.00 for 2026-2027 to intermediate districts or
7 consortia of intermediate districts to support evidence-based early
8 childhood system building as described in this section.
9 (2) From the state school aid fund money allocated in
10 subsection (1), there is allocated an amount not to exceed
11 $15,900,000.00 to intermediate districts for the purpose of
12 coordinating early childhood services to support the goals and
13 outcomes under subsections (3), (5), (7), and (8) and to provide
14 supports for early childhood programs for children prenatal through
15 age 5. The funding provided to each intermediate district under
16 this section is determined by the distribution formula established
17 by the department of lifelong education, advancement, and potential
18 for the former Great Start collaboratives. To receive funding under
19 this section, each intermediate district must provide an
20 application to the department of lifelong education, advancement,
21 and potential, by a date determined by the department of lifelong
22 education, advancement, and potential, indicating the strategies
23 planned to be provided and how the strategies align with the Help
24 Me Grow National Fidelity Assessment.
25 (3) Each intermediate district or consortium of intermediate
26 districts that receives funding under this section shall convene a
27 Help Me Grow collaborative that includes an active partnership with
28 families and multiple community-based organizations. Each
29 intermediate district is required to employ at least a 0.8 FTE Help
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
232
1 Me Grow lead responsible for local system building activities and
2 at least a 0.5 FTE care coordinator for each intermediate district
3 region. The goal of each Help Me Grow collaborative is to ensure
4 the coordination and expansion of local early childhood systems and
5 programs that achieve the following outcomes:
6 (a) Improve family access, leadership, and engagement.
7 (b) Enhance child development, learning, and well-being.
8 (c) Create strong, efficient community systems.
9 (4) The responsibilities of the intermediate district Help Me
10 Grow lead described in subsection (3) include at least the
11 following:
12 (a) Facilitating local Help Me Grow collaboratives and
13 demonstrating growth toward fidelity to the Help Me Grow model by
14 implementing the following 4 core components of the model and its
15 key activities as indicated by the Help Me Grow National Fidelity
16 Assessment:
17 (i) Centralized access point.
18 (ii) Family and community outreach.
19 (iii) Child health provider outreach.
20 (iv) Data collection and analysis.
21 (b) Ensuring that local early childhood initiatives are data
22 driven and reflect the needs of the community, including literacy,
23 home visiting, and other early childhood programs.
24 (c) Completing the annual Help Me Grow National Fidelity
25 Assessment and tracking relevant data, including referrals,
26 systems-building activities, outreach efforts, developmental
27 screenings, and barriers, to demonstrate progress and inform
28 continuous improvement.
29 (d) Providing universal access to developmental screening for
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
233
1 children birth through age 5.
2 (e) Ensuring the local intermediate district has a dedicated
3 care coordinator to support families in the community, either by
4 hiring locally, collaborating amongst intermediate districts, or
5 contracting with Help Me Grow Michigan. The care coordinator shall
6 have sufficient time dedicated to provide all of the following:
7 (i) Regular support of a public telephone line during work
8 hours to answer family questions.
9 (ii) Follow-up on universal developmental screenings and
10 referrals to appropriate early childhood programs and services.
11 (iii) Data tracking to ensure referrals from Help Me Grow
12 successfully connect to early childhood services.
13 (iv) Coordinated cross-sector supports to ensure that families
14 are seamlessly connected to the full scope of services within the
15 intermediate district region.

20 and their families. From the funds allocated in this subsection,
21 $3,500,000.00 must be distributed to intermediate districts that
22 were awarded funds under former section 32p(4) in 2024-2025 to
23 provide home visits. Any remaining funds must be distributed
24 equally to all other intermediate districts. The family support
25 services described in this subsection must be conducted as part of
26 a locally coordinated, family-centered, evidence-based, data-driven
27 strategic plan that is approved by the department of lifelong
28 education, advancement, and potential and aligned with the Help Me
29 Grow National Fidelity Assessment. The goals of the family support
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
234
1 services funded under this subsection are to improve school
2 readiness using evidence-based methods, including a focus on
3 developmentally appropriate outcomes for early literacy, to improve
4 positive parenting practices, and to improve family economic self-
5 sufficiency while reducing the impact of high-risk factors through
6 community resources and referrals. The department of lifelong
7 education, advancement, and potential shall coordinate the goals of
8 the strategic plans approved under this subsection with other state
9 agency home visit programs in a way that strengthens Michigan's
10 home visiting infrastructure and maximizes federal funds available
11 for the purposes of at-risk family support services. The
12 coordination among departments and agencies is intended to avoid
13 duplication of state services and spending, and should emphasize
14 efficient delivery of family support services.

1 $4,000,000.00 to improve literacy outcomes and advance school
2 readiness for children prenatal through age 5 by implementing
3 evidence-based practices through Help Me Grow collaboratives that

5 reading frequency, and other developmentally appropriate and
