7 A Help Me Grow collaborative may only use the funding to implement
8 family engagement practices that have an evidence base showing
9 improvement in early literacy outcomes with children prenatal
10 through age 5. Family engagements may occur through Early On
11 visits, evidence-based home visits, and Reach Out and Read well-
12 child visits. Early childhood and medical professionals who engage
13 with the child's family must have participated in evidence-based
14 training or coaching on the literacy strategies implemented. A Help
15 Me Grow collaborative that receives funds under this subsection
16 must report to the state data collected during these family
17 engagements, including at least the type of engagement, frequency
18 of engagement, coaching or training model, and outcome data. The
19 formula described in subsection (1) must be used to allocate funds
20 to intermediate districts under this subsection. If funding under
21 this subsection is insufficient to enroll all interested families
22 in the service, each intermediate district must prioritize
23 enrollment to those families with the highest levels of economic
24 need. If an intermediate district will not fully use funding
25 received under this subsection, those funds must be returned to the
26 department of lifelong education, advancement, and potential for

5 Sec. 32t. (1) From the state school aid fund money
6 appropriated in section 11, there is allocated for 2025-2026 2026-
7 2027 only an amount not to exceed $25,000,000.00 $11,000,000.00 to
8 Clinton County RESA (CCRESA) for to continue phase 2 of a preschool
9 pilot program to provide services to 3-year-old children. Eligible
10 children are those whose age is less than 4 years on September 1
11 and greater than or equal to 3 years on December 1 of the current
12 school year and whose family income is at or below 250% of the
13 federal poverty level, with priority given to families with lower
14 incomes. Additional factors such as developmental delay, language
15 barriers, or challenging behaviors may also be considered for
16 eligibility. These services must be designed for children who are
17 age 3 and must be similar to the services provided through the
18 great start readiness program. The program described in this
19 section must be administered by CCRESA Strong Beginnings
20 Implementation Team under the direction of the department of
21 lifelong education, advancement, and potential, with assessment,
22 data, and collection analysis for the program being provided by
23 Michigan State University.
24 (2) The department of lifelong education, advancement, and
25 potential must pay the funding under this section to Clinton County
26 RESA in installments over 2 years. The department of lifelong
27 education, advancement, and potential shall determine the amount to
28 be used in each year.
29 (2) (3) This section is intended to provide planning for and

1 implement continue phase 2 of the pilot preschool program for 3-
2 year-old children. The second phase of the pilot will examine
3 variations of the current program, such as part-day, 5-day-per-

5 inclusion.
6 (3) (4) In order to evaluate the outcomes and impact of strong
7 beginnings, Clinton County RESA and Michigan State University shall
8 compare outcomes for children who attend strong beginnings and the
9 great start readiness program with:
10 (a) Outcomes for children who attend the great start readiness
11 program only.
12 (b) Outcomes for children who did not attend either program.
13 (4) (5) Data used for the purpose of comparisons under
14 subsection (4) (3) must include, at a minimum, strong beginnings
15 waitlist data, strong beginnings child assessment data, the great
16 start readiness program child assessment data, kindergarten
17 attendance data, and elementary standardized testing data.

28 (6) (13) As used in this section:
29 (a) "Dyslexia" means both of the following:
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
254
1 (i) A specific learning disorder that is neurobiological in
2 origin and characterized by difficulties with accurate or fluent
3 word recognition and by poor spelling and decoding abilities that
4 typically result from a deficit in the phonological component of
5 language that is often unexpected in relation to other cognitive
6 abilities and the provision of effective classroom instruction.
7 (ii) A specific learning disorder that may include secondary
8 consequences, such as problems in reading comprehension and a
9 reduced reading experience that can impede the growth of vocabulary
10 and background knowledge and lead to social, emotional, and
11 behavioral difficulties.
12 (b) "Evidence-based" means an activity, program, process,
13 service, strategy, or intervention that demonstrates statistically
14 significant effects on improving pupil outcomes or other relevant
15 outcomes and that meets at least both of the following:
16 (i) At least 1 of the following:
17 (A) Is based on strong evidence from at least 1 well-designed
18 and well-implemented experimental study.
19 (B) Is based on moderate evidence from at least 1 well-
20 designed and well-implemented quasi-experimental study.
21 (C) Is based on promising evidence from at least 1 well-
22 designed and well-implemented correlational study with statistical
23 controls for selection bias.
24 (D) Demonstrates a rationale based on high-quality research
25 findings or positive evaluation that the activity, program,
26 process, service, strategy, or intervention is likely to improve
27 pupil outcomes or other relevant outcomes.
28 (ii) Includes ongoing efforts to examine the effects of the
29 activity, program, process, service, strategy, or intervention.
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
255
1 (c) "Explicit" means direct and deliberate instruction through
2 continuous pupil-teacher interaction that includes teacher
3 modeling, guided practice, and independent practice.
4 (d) "Fluency" means the ability to read with speed, accuracy,
5 and proper expression.
6 (e) "Multi-tiered system of supports" means a comprehensive
7 framework that includes 3 distinct tiers of instructional support
8 and is composed of a collection of evidence-based strategies
9 designed to meet the individual needs and assets of a whole pupil
10 at all achievement levels.
11 (f) "Phonemic awareness" means the conscious awareness of all
12 of the following:
13 (i) Individual speech sounds, including, but not limited to,
14 consonants and vowels, in spoken syllables.
15 (ii) The ability to consciously manipulate through, including,
16 but not limited to, matching, blending, segmenting, deleting, or
17 substituting, individual speech sounds described in subparagraph
18 (i).
19 (iii) All levels of the speech sound system, including, but not
20 limited to, word boundaries, rhyme recognition, stress patterns,
21 syllables, onset-rime units, and phonemes.
22 (g) "Phonological" means relating to the system of contrastive
23 relationships among the speech sounds that constitute the
24 fundamental components of a language.
25 (h) "Progress monitoring" means the assessing of students'
26 academic performance, quantifying students' rates of improvement or
27 progress toward goals, and determining how students are responding
28 to instruction.
29 (i) "Rapid automatized naming (RAN)" means a task that
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
256
1 measures how quickly individuals can name objects; pictures;
2 colors; or symbols, including letters and digits, aloud, which can
3 predict later reading abilities for preliterate children.
4 Sec. 35e. (1) From the state school aid fund money
5 appropriated in section 11, there is allocated for 2025-2026 only
6 an amount not to exceed $250,000.00 to Wayne State University to
7 study this state's public school library programs.
8 (2) Wayne State University shall convene a committee to
9 conduct the study described in this section. The committee must
10 consist of 1 member from each of the following organizations:
11 (a) The department.
12 (b) The Library of Michigan.
13 (c) The Michigan Association of School Librarians.
14 (d) The Michigan senate.
15 (e) The Michigan house of representatives.
16 (f) The Michigan Education Association.
17 (g) The Michigan chapter of the American Federation of
18 Teachers.
19 (h) The Michigan Association of Superintendents and
20 Administrators.
21 (i) A Michigan research university.
22 (3) The committee described in subsection (2) shall conduct a
23 study to investigate at least all of the following issues:
24 (a) How many schools in each district have a school library.
25 (b) What hours school libraries are open each week for
26 students and faculty to use.
27 (c) What full-time equivalency of certified school librarians
28 are employed at each building.
29 (d) The ratio of students per certified school librarian.
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
257
1 (e) What full-time equivalency of paraprofessional or other
2 staff are employed in the school library and the credentialing of
3 these staff, if any.
4 (f) How school libraries are scheduled.
5 (g) How many hours each week school librarians provide direct
6 library-related instruction to students.
7 (h) The technology available for students to access library
8 resources and lessons.
9 (i) The size and age of the collection in each school library,
10 and the extent of digital materials available for students to
11 access.
12 (j) Current funding per student for school library materials.
13 (k) Any other matters that the committee considers relevant to
14 the fulfillment of its mission to determine the status of school
15 library programs in this state.
16 (4) The department shall provide staff and other resources as
17 the committee described in subsection (2) considers appropriate,
18 including contracting with a researcher. Appropriate costs must be
19 determined by the department, and the committee described in
20 subsection (2) shall reimburse the department for costs related to
21 this subsection.
22 (5) The committee described in subsection (2) may conduct
23 public hearings to gather information, and may sponsor statewide or
24 regional conferences involving educators, students, or the public
25 at large.
26 (6) The committee described in subsection (2) shall provide a
27 report to the house and senate appropriations subcommittees on
28 school aid, the state budget director, the house and senate fiscal

17 Sec. 35m. (1) From the state school aid fund money

19 2027 only an amount not to exceed $64,400,000.00 $100,000,000.00
20 for the purposes described in this section, including payments to
21 improve educational outcomes in literacy. The department shall
22 award funding allocated for 2026-2027 under this section in a
23 quantity and magnitude such that the allocation in this subsection
24 is distributed over 2 fiscal years.
25 (2) The department shall continue the committee for literacy
26 achievement that includes representatives of higher education and
27 early childhood literacy educators who have expertise in literacy
28 instruction and research. The committee shall exist until at least
29 September 2029. The department shall appoint the members of the
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
259
1 committee for literacy achievement. The department shall develop a
2 process to identify and assemble experts for the committee. The
3 primary focus of the committee is to recommend tools and strategies
4 with the goal of increasing student academic outcomes in the area
5 of literacy. The committee is encouraged to collaborate with the
6 Michigan Education Research Institute or other entities with
7 similar expertise on the subject of literacy. The department shall
8 determine the size and benchmarks of the committee. The committee
9 shall do all of the following:
10 (a) Critically evaluate early literacy series and materials.
11 The committee shall evaluate with research-based outcomes, using
12 the committee's expertise in the field of literacy. The committee
13 shall assign grade tiers to the series based on the series'
14 likelihood to increase student literacy outcomes. Priority must be
15 given to series that already have a demonstrated history of
16 increasing student outcomes. The committee may perform an
17 evaluation under this subdivision based on submissions from a
18 vendor, but must independently verify the validity of information
19 provided by the vendor. The committee is also strongly encouraged
20 to evaluate available materials not submitted to the department by
21 a vendor.
22 (b) Critically evaluate literacy professional development. The
23 committee shall evaluate with research-based outcomes, using the
24 committee's expertise in the field of literacy. The committee shall
25 assign grade tiers to the professional development based on the
26 professional development's likelihood to increase student literacy
27 outcomes. Priority must be given to professional development that
28 already has a demonstrated history of increasing student outcomes.
29 The committee may perform an evaluation under this subdivision
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
260
1 based on submissions from a vendor, but must independently verify
2 the validity of information provided by the vendor. The committee
3 is also strongly encouraged to evaluate available materials not
4 submitted to the department by a vendor.
5 (c) Critically evaluate other applicable literacy tools or
6 services the committee determines to have a high likelihood or
7 demonstrated history of increasing student literacy outcomes. The
8 committee may perform an evaluation under this subdivision based on
9 submissions from a vendor, but must independently verify the
10 validity of information provided by the vendor. The committee is
11 also strongly encouraged to evaluate available materials not
12 submitted to the department by a vendor.
13 (d) Create, maintain, and post annually on a publicly
14 available website a rankings list of all early literacy series and
15 other items the committee has evaluated. This includes items from
16 each part of the rankings list, items evaluated in the 2024-2025
17 school year, and any subsequent items evaluated going forward. The
18 committee shall denote on the list whether major changes have been
19 made to an early literacy series or other item on the list since
20 the early literacy series or other item was evaluated by the
21 committee and if the early literacy series or other item has not
22 been evaluated since the last ranking. The rankings list created in
23 this subdivision must align with the list of evidence-based tier 1,
24 classwide elementary reading curricula and materials that are
25 aligned with science of reading methods that research has shown to
26 improve literacy outcomes and help pupils achieve reading
27 proficiency as required under section 1280f of the revised school
28 code, MCL 380.1280f.
29 (3) An individual on the committee shall recuse themselves
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
261
1 from evaluating early literacy series and other items that the
2 individual has helped create, that the individual would benefit
3 from financially, or for which the individual has any other
4 conflict of interest.

12 in 2026-2027. 2027-2028. The department shall use the rankings list

7 (2) Eligible tutoring partners shall apply for funding in a
8 form and manner determined by the department. An application for
9 funding described in this subsection must include at least all of
10 the following:
11 (a) An identification of a district or intermediate district
12 that will serve as a fiscal agent for awarded funds.

15 a letter of support from each district that affirms their
16 commitment to ensure alignment with tier 1 instruction,
17 implementation fidelity, including, but not limited to, student
18 usage and frequency, and participation in data sharing and training
19 necessary to maximize outcomes.
20 (c) Projections for the number of students who will be served,
21 broken down by partner district.
22 (d) A description of the efficacious nature of the eligible
23 tutoring partner's program, including the ability of the eligible
24 tutoring partner to hit certain benchmarks and goals, based on
25 evidence.
26 (e) A plan for how the eligible tutoring partner will target
27 high-impact tutoring for the highest need student populations based
28 on some or all of the following criteria:
29 (i) Statewide assessment data.

269
1 (ii) Screeners or formative assessment scores.
2 (iii) Economically disadvantaged status.
3 (iv) Special education status.
4 (v) English language learner status.
5 (f) A planned budget for the high-impact tutoring program
6 provided, including the total dollars necessary to serve the
7 identified student population and an estimate of the percentage of
8 awarded dollars that would be directly deployed for high-impact
9 tutoring.
10 (g) A commitment to provide periodic reporting to each
11 district served and to the department, in a form and manner

17 (i) Any additional information required by the department to

25 budget, and the demonstrated outcome capacity of the high-impact
26 tutoring program.

28 be able to meet the stated outcome benchmarks.

CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

1 grants are awarded to the most impactful applicants.

7 educational progress (M-STEP).

6 in this section, in-person tutoring that meets all of the
7 following:
8 (i) Occurs during the school day.
9 (ii) Is individualized, with tutor ratios not higher than 1:4.
10 (iii) Provides intensive support with a minimum of 2 sessions
11 per week.
12 (iv) Uses a structured, evidence-based curriculum designed for
13 tutoring, with formative assessments to track progress.
14 (v) Uses trained tutors.

10 determines that it is able to include additional eligible children
11 in the great start readiness program without additional funds under
12 section 32d, the applicant may include additional eligible children
13 but does not receive additional funding under section 32d for those
14 children.
15 (8) The department of lifelong education, advancement, and
16 potential shall review the program components under section 32d and
17 under this section at least biennially. The department of lifelong
18 education, advancement, and potential also shall convene a
19 committee of internal and external stakeholders at least once every
20 5 years to ensure that the funding structure under this section
21 reflects current system needs under section 32d.

23 (a) Speech and language impairment, 1.1.

1 (h) Deaf-blindness, 3.1.

12 Sec. 53a. (1) For districts, reimbursement for pupils
13 described in subsection (2) is 100% of the total approved costs of
14 operating special education programs and services approved by the
15 department and included in the intermediate district plan adopted
16 under article 3 of the revised school code, MCL 380.1701 to
17 380.1761, minus the district's foundation allowance calculated
18 under section 20 and minus the district's per-pupil allocation
19 under section 20m. For intermediate districts, the department shall
20 calculate reimbursement for pupils described in subsection (2) in
21 the same manner as for a district, using the foundation allowance
22 under section 20 of the pupil's district of residence, not to
23 exceed the target foundation allowance under section 20 for the
24 current fiscal year plus the amount of the district's per-pupil
25 allocation under section 20m.
26 (2) Reimbursement under subsection (1) is for the following
27 special education pupils:
28 (a) Pupils assigned to a district or intermediate district
29 through the community placement program of the courts or a state
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
294
1 agency, if the pupil was a resident of another intermediate
2 district at the time the pupil came under the jurisdiction of the
3 court or a state agency.
4 (b) Pupils who are residents of institutions operated by the
5 department of health and human services.
6 (c) Pupils who are former residents of department of community
7 health institutions for the developmentally disabled who are placed
8 in community settings other than the pupil's home.
9 (d) Pupils enrolled in a department-approved on-grounds
10 educational program longer than 180 days, but not longer than 233
11 days, at a residential child care institution, if the child care
12 institution offered in 1991-92 an on-grounds educational program
13 longer than 180 days but not longer than 233 days.
14 (e) Pupils placed in a district by a parent for the purpose of
15 seeking a suitable home, if the parent does not reside in the same
16 intermediate district as the district in which the pupil is placed.
17 (3) Only those costs that are clearly and directly
18 attributable to educational programs for pupils described in
19 subsection (2), and that would not have been incurred if the pupils
20 were not being educated in a district or intermediate district, are
21 reimbursable under this section.

1 (9) Funds distributed under this section may be used to fund

20 is on track to graduate.

1 (b) Create, maintain, and enhance this state's P-20

18 improve teaching and learning in the classroom.

20 research that advances this state's educational performance.

22 policymakers to make informed policy decisions.

1 achievement.

7 and postsecondary, meets all of the following:
8 (i) Contains a unique statewide student identifier that does
9 not permit a student to be individually identified by users of the
10 system, except as allowed by federal and state law.
11 (ii) Contains student-level enrollment, demographic, and
12 program participation information, including data associated with
13 students who have been identified as having an affiliation to 1 or
14 more federally recognized Indian tribes and student participation
15 in federal programs funded under 20 USC 7401 to 7546 and
16 participation in federal programs funded under the Johnson-O'Malley
17 Supplemental supplemental Indian Education Program Modernization
18 Act, education program modernization act, Public Law 115-404. Any
19 reports or data access related specifically to tribal affiliation
20 must be done through ongoing consultation with the federally
21 recognized tribes in the state with the expectation that the
22 center, the department, and the tribes will work iteratively toward
23 meaningful reports, access, and use of these records to improve
24 shared education interests and outcomes.
25 (iii) Contains student-level information about the points at
26 which students exit, transfer in, transfer out, drop out, or
27 complete education programs.
28 (iv) Has the capacity to communicate with higher education data
29 systems.

1 (i) For data elements related to preschool through grade 12

20 Virtual Learning Research Institute. The Michigan Virtual Learning
21 Research Institute shall do all of the following:
22 (a) Support and accelerate innovation in education through the
23 following activities:
24 (i) Test, evaluate, and recommend as appropriate new
25 technology-based instructional tools and resources.

28 multimedia instructional content.

CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
336
1 department criteria by which cyber schools and virtual course

7 of virtual learning delivery models in preparing pupils to be
8 college- and career-ready and publish a report that highlights
9 enrollment totals, completion rates, and the overall impact on
10 pupils. The Michigan Virtual Learning Research Institute shall

14 intermediate districts by not later than March 31 of each fiscal
15 year for which funding is allocated under this section.

18 administrators, and school board members, that focuses on the

17 (12) As used in this section:
18 (a) "Blended learning" means a hybrid instructional delivery
19 model where pupils are provided content, instruction, and
20 assessment, in part at a supervised educational facility away from
21 home where the pupil and a teacher with a valid Michigan teaching
22 certificate are in the same physical location and in part through
23 internet-connected learning environments with some degree of pupil
24 control over time, location, and pace of instruction.
25 (b) "Cyber school" means a full-time instructional program of
26 virtual courses for pupils that may or may not require attendance
27 at a physical school location.
28 (c) "Virtual course" means a course of study that is capable
29 of generating a credit or a grade and that is provided in an
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
344
1 interactive learning environment in which the majority of the
2 curriculum is delivered using the internet and in which pupils are
3 separated from their instructor or teacher of record by time or
4 location, or both.

345
1 infrastructure, content, and engagement of the MLC in partnership

1 under section 11, there is allocated for 2026-2027 an amount not to

6 the number of pupils who are college- and career-ready upon high
7 school graduation, and to promote certificate and degree attainment

11 (2) The MiSTEM council annually shall review and make
12 recommendations to the governor, the legislature, and the
13 department concerning changes to the statewide strategy adopted by
14 the council for delivering STEM education-related opportunities to
15 pupils. The MiSTEM council shall use funds received under this
16 section to ensure that the MiSTEM council's members or their
17 designees are trained in a third-party evaluation system such as
18 the Change the Equation STEMworks program for the purpose of rating
19 STEM programs.
20 (3) The MiSTEM council shall make specific funding
21 recommendations for the funds allocated under subsection (4) by
22 December 15 of each fiscal year. Each specific funding
23 recommendation must be for a program or demonstration grant
24 approved by the MiSTEM council. All of the following apply:
25 (a) To be eligible for MiSTEM council approval as described in
26 this subsection, a program must satisfy all of the following:
27 (i) Align with this state's academic standards.
28 (ii) Have STEMworks or other third-party certification.
29 (iii) Provide project-based experiential learning, student
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
359
1 programming, or educator professional learning experiences.
2 (iv) Focus predominantly on classroom-based STEM experiences or
3 professional learning experiences.
4 (b) The MiSTEM council shall approve programs that represent
5 all network regions and include a diverse array of options for
6 students and educators.
7 (c) To be eligible for MiSTEM council approval as described in
8 this subsection, a demonstration grant must satisfy all of the
9 following:
10 (i) Align with this state's academic standards.
11 (ii) In collaboration with the department, scale project,
12 problem, or place-based learning in a building or district in
13 support of the statewide STEM strategy as recommended by the MiSTEM
14 council.
15 (d) The MiSTEM council is encouraged to work with the MiSTEM
16 network to develop locally and regionally developed programs,
17 demonstration grants, and professional learning experiences for the
18 programs on the list of approved programs. At the direction of the
19 MiSTEM council, funds can be used to evaluate and market regions,
20 programs, and demonstration grants.
21 (e) If the MiSTEM council is unable to make specific funding
22 recommendations by December 15 of a fiscal year, the department
23 shall award and distribute the funds allocated under subsection (4)
24 on a competitive grant basis that at least follows the statewide
25 STEM strategy plan and rating system recommended by the MiSTEM
26 council. Each grant must provide STEM education-related
27 opportunities for pupils.

1 exceed $3,800,000.00 for the purpose of funding programs or

14 (6) A MiSTEM network region shall do all of the following:
15 (a) Collaborate with the career and educational advisory
16 council that is located in the MiSTEM region and the department to
17 develop a regional strategic plan for STEM education that creates a
18 robust regional STEM culture, that empowers STEM teachers, that
19 integrates business and education into the STEM network, and that
20 ensures high-quality STEM experiences for pupils. At a minimum, a
21 regional STEM strategic plan described in this subdivision should
22 do all of the following in collaboration with the department:
23 (i) Identify regional employer need for STEM.
24 (ii) Identify processes for regional employers and educators to
25 create guided pathways for STEM careers that include internships or
26 externships, apprenticeships, and other experiential engagements
27 for pupils.

29 including internships or externships and apprenticeships, that
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
361
1 integrate this state's science standards into high-quality STEM
2 experiences that engage pupils.

4 employer networking and STEM career fairs to raise STEM awareness.

6 MiSTEM network functions to further the mission of STEM in this
7 state in coordination with the MiSTEM council and the department of
8 labor and economic opportunity.
9 (d) Facilitate application and implementation of state and
10 federal funds under this subsection and any other grants or funds
11 for the MiSTEM network region.
12 (e) Work with districts to provide STEM programming and
13 professional learning.
14 (f) Coordinate recurring discussions and work with the career
15 and educational advisory council to ensure that feedback and best
16 practices are being shared, including funding, program,
17 professional learning opportunities, and regional strategic plans.
18 (g) In collaboration with the department, deploy marketing and
19 outreach efforts to publish the availability of STEM learning
20 opportunities.

2 Sec. 101. (1) To be eligible to receive state aid under this
3 article, not later than the fifth Wednesday after the pupil
4 membership count day and not later than the fifth Wednesday after
5 the supplemental count day, each district superintendent shall
6 submit and certify to the center and the intermediate
7 superintendent, in the form and manner prescribed by the center,
8 the number of pupils enrolled and in regular daily attendance,
9 including identification of tuition-paying pupils, in the district
10 as of the pupil membership count day and as of the supplemental
11 count day, as applicable, for the current school year. In addition,
12 a district maintaining school during the entire year shall submit
13 and certify to the center and the intermediate superintendent, in
14 the form and manner prescribed by the center, the number of pupils
15 enrolled and in regular daily attendance in the district for the
16 current school year pursuant to rules promulgated by the
17 superintendent. Not later than the sixth Wednesday after the pupil
18 membership count day and not later than the sixth Wednesday after
19 the supplemental count day, the district shall resolve any pupil
20 membership conflicts with another district, correct any data
21 issues, and recertify the data in a form and manner prescribed by
22 the center and file the certified data with the intermediate
23 superintendent. If a district fails to submit and certify the
24 attendance data, as required under this subsection, the center
25 shall notify the department and the department shall withhold state
26 aid due to be distributed under this article from the defaulting
27 district immediately, beginning with the next payment after the
28 failure and continuing with each payment until the district
29 complies with this subsection. If a district does not comply with
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
364
1 this subsection by the end of the fiscal year, the district
2 forfeits the amount withheld. A person who willfully falsifies a
3 figure or statement in the certified and sworn copy of enrollment
4 is subject to penalty as prescribed by section 161.
5 (2) To be eligible to receive state aid under this article,
6 not later than the twenty-fourth Wednesday after the pupil
7 membership count day and not later than the twenty-fourth Wednesday
8 after the supplemental count day, an intermediate district shall
9 submit to the center, in a form and manner prescribed by the
10 center, the audited enrollment and attendance data as described in
11 subsection (1) for the pupils of its constituent districts and of
12 the intermediate district. If an intermediate district fails to
13 submit the audited data as required under this subsection, the
14 department shall withhold state aid due to be distributed under
15 this article from the defaulting intermediate district immediately,
16 beginning with the next payment after the failure and continuing
17 with each payment until the intermediate district complies with
18 this subsection. If an intermediate district does not comply with
19 this subsection by the end of the fiscal year, the intermediate
20 district forfeits the amount withheld.
21 (3) Except as otherwise provided in subsections (11) and (12)
22 all of the following apply to the provision of pupil instruction:
23 (a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, each
24 district shall provide at least 1,098 hours and 180 days of pupil
25 instruction. If a collective bargaining agreement that provides a
26 complete school calendar was in effect for employees of a district
27 as of June 24, 2014, and if that school calendar is not in
28 compliance with this subdivision, then this subdivision does not
29 apply to that district until after the expiration of that
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
365
1 collective bargaining agreement. A district may apply for a waiver
2 under subsection (9) or subdivision (h) from the requirements of
3 this subdivision.
4 (b) Except as otherwise provided in this article, a district
5 failing to comply with the required minimum hours and days of pupil
6 instruction under this subsection forfeits from its total state aid
7 allocation an amount determined by applying a ratio of the number
8 of hours or days the district was in noncompliance in relation to
9 the required minimum number of hours and days under this
10 subsection. Not later than the first business day in August, the
11 board of each district shall either certify to the department that
12 the district was in full compliance with this section regarding the
13 number of hours and days of pupil instruction in the previous
14 school year, or report to the department, in a form and manner
15 prescribed by the center, each instance of noncompliance. If the
16 district did not provide at least the required minimum number of
17 hours and days of pupil instruction under this subsection, the
18 department shall make the deduction of state aid in the following
19 fiscal year from the first payment of state school aid. A district
20 is not subject to forfeiture of funds under this subsection for a
21 fiscal year in which a forfeiture was already imposed under
22 subsection (6).
23 (c) Hours or days lost because of strikes or teachers'
24 conferences are not counted as hours or days of pupil instruction.
25 (d) Except as otherwise provided in subdivisions (e) and (f),
26 this subsection, if a district does not have at least 75% of the
27 district's membership in attendance on any day of pupil
28 instruction, the department shall pay the district state aid in
29 that proportion of 1/180 that the actual percent of attendance
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
366
1 bears to 75%.
2 (e) If a district adds 1 or more days of pupil instruction to
3 the end of its instructional calendar for a school year to comply
4 with subdivision (a) because the district otherwise would fail to
5 provide the required minimum number of days of pupil instruction
6 even after the operation of subsection (4) due to conditions not
7 within the control of school authorities, then subdivision (d) does
8 not apply for any day of pupil instruction that is added to the end
9 of the instructional calendar. Instead, for any of those days, if
10 the district does not have at least 60% of the district's
11 membership in attendance on that day, the department shall pay the
12 district state aid in that proportion of 1/180 that the actual
13 percentage of attendance bears to 60%. For any day of pupil
14 instruction added to the instructional calendar as described in
15 this subdivision, the district shall report to the department the
16 percentage of the district's membership that is in attendance, in
17 the form and manner prescribed by the department.
18 (f) At the request of a district that operates a department-
19 approved alternative education program and that does not provide
20 instruction for pupils in all of grades K to 12, the superintendent
21 shall grant a waiver from the requirements of subdivision (d). The
22 waiver must provide that an eligible district is subject to the
23 proration provisions of subdivision (d) only if the district does
24 not have at least 50% of the district's membership in attendance on
25 any day of pupil instruction. In order to be eligible for this
26 waiver, a district must maintain records to substantiate its
27 compliance with the following requirements:
28 (i) The district offers the minimum hours of pupil instruction
29 as required under this section.
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
367
1 (ii) For each enrolled pupil, the district uses appropriate
2 academic assessments to develop an individual education plan that
3 leads to a high school diploma.

5 progress at regular intervals and records the results of those
6 tests in that pupil's individual education plan.
7 (g) All of the following apply to a waiver granted under
8 subdivision (f):
9 (i) If the waiver is for a blended model of delivery, a waiver
10 that is granted for the 2011-2012 fiscal year or a subsequent

22 (i) or (ii) is valid for 3 fiscal years, unless it is revoked by the
23 superintendent, and must be renewed at the end of the 3-year period
24 to remain in effect.

1 that is covered by a state of emergency declared by the governor.

11 (4) All of the following apply to the counting of days and
12 hours of pupil instruction under this section:
13 (a) Except as otherwise provided in this subsection, the first
14 6 days or the equivalent number of hours for which pupil
15 instruction is not provided because of conditions not within the
16 control of school authorities, such as severe storms, fires,
17 epidemics, utility power unavailability, water or sewer failure, or
18 health conditions as defined by the city, county, or state health
19 authorities, are counted as hours and days of pupil instruction.
20 (b) With the approval of the superintendent of public
21 instruction, the department shall count as hours and days of pupil
22 instruction for a fiscal year not more than 3 additional days or
23 the equivalent number of additional hours for which pupil
24 instruction is not provided in a district due to unusual and
25 extenuating occurrences resulting from conditions not within the
26 control of school authorities such as those conditions described in
27 this subsection.
28 (c) A district that counts hours or days of professional
29 development for teachers as hours or days of pupil instruction, as
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
370
1 provided under subsection (10), is eligible to have additional
2 hours or days counted as hours and days of pupil instruction as
3 provided under subdivision (b) to the same extent as a district
4 that does not count hours or days of professional development for
5 teachers as hours or days of pupil instruction.
6 (d) In deciding whether or not to approve the counting of
7 additional hours or days of pupil instruction under subdivision (b)
8 for a district, the superintendent of public instruction shall not
9 take into account whether or not the district counts hours or days
10 of professional development for teachers as hours or days of pupil
11 instruction, as provided under subsection (10).
12 (e) Subsequent hours or days beyond those described in
13 subdivisions (a) and (b) are not counted as hours or days of pupil
14 instruction.

5 (10) A district may count up to 38 hours of professional
6 development for teachers as hours of pupil instruction. All of the
7 following apply to the counting of professional development as
8 pupil instruction under this subsection:
9 (a) If the professional development exceeds 5 hours in a
10 single day, that day may be counted as a day of pupil instruction.
11 (b) At least 8 hours of the professional development counted
12 as hours of pupil instruction under this subsection must be
13 recommended by a districtwide professional development advisory
14 committee appointed by the district board. The advisory committee
15 must be composed of teachers employed by the district who represent
16 a variety of grades and subject matter specializations, including
17 special education; nonteaching staff; parents; and administrators.
18 The majority membership of the committee must be composed of
19 teaching staff.
20 (c) Professional development provided online is allowable and
21 encouraged, as long as the instruction has been approved by the
22 district. The department shall issue a list of approved online
23 professional development providers that must include the Michigan
24 Virtual School.
25 (d) Professional development may only be counted as hours of
26 pupil instruction under this subsection for the pupils of those
27 teachers scheduled to participate in the professional development.
28 (e) The professional development must meet all of the
29 following to be counted as pupil instruction under this subsection:
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
375
1 (i) Be aligned to the school or district improvement plan for
2 the school or district in which the professional development is
3 being provided.
4 (ii) Be linked to 1 or more criteria in the evaluation tool
5 developed or adopted by the district or intermediate district under
6 section 1249 of the revised school code, MCL 380.1249.
7 (iii) Has been approved by the department as counting for state
8 continuing education clock hours. The number of hours of
9 professional development counted as hours of pupil instruction
10 under this subsection may not exceed the number of state continuing
11 education clock hours for which the professional development was
12 approved.
13 (iv) Not more than a combined total of 10 hours of the
14 professional development takes place before the first scheduled day
15 of school for the school year ending in the fiscal year and after
16 the last scheduled day of school for that school year.
17 (v) Not more than 10 hours of the professional development
18 takes place in a single month.
19 (vi) At least 75% of teachers scheduled to participate in the
20 professional development are in attendance.

1 department before enrolling an individual to determine the

5 that are incidental to teaching and the provision of educational
6 services to nonpublic school students; that are noninstructional in

11 ensuring the health, safety, and welfare of the children in
12 nonpublic schools and to reimburse nonpublic schools for costs
13 described in this section.
14 (8) Funds allocated under this section are not intended to aid
15 or maintain any nonpublic school, support the attendance of any
16 student at a nonpublic school, employ any person at a nonpublic
17 school, support the attendance of any student at any location where
18 instruction is offered to a nonpublic school student, or support
19 the employment of any person at any location where instruction is
20 offered to a nonpublic school student.
21 (9) For purposes of this section, "actual cost" means the
22 hourly wage for the employee or employees performing a task or
23 tasks required to comply with a health, safety, or welfare
24 requirement under a law or administrative rule of this state
25 identified by the department under subsection (2) and is to be
26 calculated in accordance with the form published by the department
27 under subsection (2), which must include a detailed itemization of
28 costs. The nonpublic school shall not charge more than the hourly
29 wage of its lowest-paid employee capable of performing a specific
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
407
1 task regardless of whether that individual is available and
2 regardless of who actually performs a specific task. Labor costs
3 under this subsection must be estimated and charged in increments
4 of 15 minutes or more, with all partial time increments rounded
5 down. When calculating costs under subsection (4), fee components
6 must be itemized in a manner that expresses both the hourly wage
7 and the number of hours charged. The nonpublic school may not
8 charge any applicable labor charge amount to cover or partially
9 cover the cost of health or fringe benefits. A nonpublic school
10 shall not charge any overtime wages in the calculation of labor
11 costs.
12 (10) Training fees, inspection fees, and criminal background
13 check fees are considered actual costs in complying with a health,
14 safety, or welfare requirement under a law or administrative rule
15 of this state.
16 (11) The funds allocated under this section for 2025-2026
17 2026-2027 are a work project appropriation, and any unexpended
18 funds for 2025-2026 2026-2027 are carried forward into 2026-2027.
19 2027-2028. The purpose of the work project is to continue to
20 reimburse nonpublic schools for actual costs incurred in complying
21 with a health, safety, or welfare requirement mandated by a law or
22 administrative rule of this state. The estimated completion date of
23 the work project is September 30, 2027.2028.
24 (12) The department shall reimburse nonpublic schools for
25 actual costs incurred in complying with health, safety, or welfare
26 requirements under a law or administrative rule of this state from
27 2017-2018 through 2022-2023 using work project funds or, if those
28 funds are insufficient to fund reimbursements under this
29 subsection, from the allocation under subsection (1).

11 Sec. 163. (1) Except as otherwise provided in the revised
12 school code, the board of a district or intermediate district shall
13 not permit any of the following:
14 (a) An individual who is not appropriately placed under a
15 valid certificate, valid substitute permit, authorization, or
16 approval issued under rules promulgated by the department to teach
17 in an elementary or secondary school.
18 (b) An individual who does not satisfy the requirements of
19 section 1233 of the revised school code, MCL 380.1233, and rules
20 promulgated by the department to provide school counselor services
21 to pupils in an elementary or secondary school.
22 (b) (c) An individual who does not satisfy the requirements of
23 section 1246 of the revised school code, MCL 380.1246, and rules
24 promulgated by the department to be employed as a superintendent,
25 principal, or assistant principal, or as an individual whose
26 primary responsibility is to administer instructional programs in
27 an elementary or secondary school or in a district or intermediate
28 district, unless the individual is working under a valid substitute
29 permit issued under rules promulgated by the department.

1 as grants or direct appropriation grants, funded under the

10 (2) Notwithstanding any other conditions or requirements for
11 direct appropriation grants, the responsible entity shall perform,
12 at a minimum, at least all of the following activities to
13 administer the grants described in subsection (1):
14 (a) Establish a process to review, complete, and execute a
15 grant agreement with a grant recipient. The responsible entity
16 shall not execute a grant agreement unless all necessary
17 documentation has been submitted and reviewed.
18 (b) Verify to the extent possible that a grant recipient is a
19 not-for-profit entity and will use funds as publicly disclosed and
20 for a public purpose that serves the economic prosperity, health,
21 safety, or general welfare of the residents of this state.
22 (c) Review and verify all necessary information to ensure the
23 grant recipient is reasonably able to execute the grant agreement,
24 perform its fiduciary duty, and comply with all applicable state
25 and federal statutes. The responsible entity may deduct the cost of
26 background checks and any other efforts performed as part of this
27 verification from the amount of the designated grant award.
28 (d) Disburse the grant money per the grant disbursement
29 schedule in the executed grant agreement on a reimbursement basis
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma
415
1 after the grantee has provided sufficient documentation, as
2 determined by the responsible entity, to verify that expenditures
3 were made in accordance with the project purpose.
4 (e) If the state budget director determines that information
5 provided by the grantee does not meet the disclosure requirements,
6 that the grant will be used to pay a tax lien, delinquent tax, or
7 other obligation owed to this state, or that the grant will create
8 a conflict of interest, the responsible entity shall not release
9 the grant money to the grantee. Money that is not released under
10 this subdivision lapses at the end of the fiscal year. There is not
11 a conflict of interest if the sponsoring legislator certifies that
12 the sponsoring legislator's immediate family members, legislative
13 staff members that have worked for the sponsoring legislator within
14 the past 2 years, and the sponsoring legislator do not have a
15 direct or indirect pecuniary interest in the legislatively directed
16 spending item.

19 of the following:
20 (a) All necessary identifying information for the grant

23 section.

25 will be expended, including tentative timelines and the estimated

416
1 stated in the executed grant agreement, from appropriations under

THE PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF MICHIGAN ENACT:
1 Sec. 6. (1) "Center program" means a program operated by a
2 district or by an intermediate district for special education
3 pupils from several districts in programs for pupils with autism
4 spectrum disorder, pupils with severe cognitive impairment, pupils
5 with moderate cognitive impairment, pupils with severe multiple
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

3
1 impairments, pupils with hearing impairment, pupils with visual
2 impairment, and pupils with physical impairment or other health
3 impairment. Programs for pupils with emotional impairment housed in
4 buildings that do not serve regular education pupils also qualify.
5 Unless otherwise approved by the department, a center program
6 either serves all constituent districts within an intermediate
7 district or serves several districts with less than 50% of the
8 pupils residing in the operating district. In addition, special
9 education center program pupils placed part-time in noncenter
10 programs to comply with the least restrictive environment
11 provisions of section 1412 of the individuals with disabilities
12 education act, 20 USC 1412, may be considered center program pupils
13 for pupil accounting purposes for the time scheduled in either a
14 center program or a noncenter program.
15 (2) "District and high school graduation rate" means the
16 annual completion and pupil dropout rate that is calculated by the
17 center pursuant to nationally recognized standards.
18 (3) "District and high school graduation report" means a
19 report of the number of pupils, excluding adult education
20 participants, in the district for the immediately preceding school
21 year, adjusted for those pupils who have transferred into or out of
22 the district or high school, who leave high school with a diploma
23 or other credential of equal status.
24 (4) "Membership", except as otherwise provided in this
25 article, means for a district, a public school academy, or an
26 intermediate district the sum of the product of .90 times the
27 number of full-time equated pupils in grades K to 12 actually
28 enrolled and in regular daily attendance in the district, public
29 school academy, or intermediate district on the pupil membership
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

4
1 count day for the current school year, plus the product of .10
2 times the final audited count from the supplemental count day of
3 full-time equated pupils in grades K to 12 actually enrolled and in
4 regular daily attendance in the district, public school academy, or
5 intermediate district for the immediately preceding school year. A
6 district's, public school academy's, or intermediate district's
7 membership is adjusted as provided under section 25e for pupils who
8 enroll after the pupil membership count day in a strict discipline
9 academy operating under sections 1311b to 1311m of the revised
10 school code, MCL 380.1311b to 380.1311m. All pupil counts used in
11 this subsection are as determined by the department and calculated
12 by adding the number of pupils registered for attendance plus
13 pupils received by transfer and minus pupils lost as defined by
14 rules promulgated by the superintendent, and as corrected by a
15 subsequent department audit. The amount of the foundation allowance
16 for a pupil in membership is determined under section 20. In making
17 the calculation of membership, all of the following, as applicable,
18 apply to determining the membership of a district, a public school
19 academy, or an intermediate district:
20 (a) Except as otherwise provided in this subsection, and
21 pursuant to subsection (6), a pupil is counted in membership in the
22 pupil's educating district or districts. Except as otherwise
23 provided in this subsection, an individual pupil must not be
24 counted for more than a total of 1.0 full-time equated membership.
25 (b) If a pupil is educated in a district other than the
26 pupil's district of residence, if the pupil is not being educated
27 as part of a cooperative education program, if the pupil's district
28 of residence does not give the educating district its approval to
29 count the pupil in membership in the educating district, and if the
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

5
1 pupil is not covered by an exception specified in subsection (6) to
2 the requirement that the educating district must have the approval
3 of the pupil's district of residence to count the pupil in
4 membership, the pupil is not counted in membership in any district.
5 (c) A special education pupil educated by the intermediate
6 district is counted in membership in the intermediate district.
7 (d) A pupil placed by a court or state agency in an on-grounds
8 program of a juvenile detention facility, a child caring
9 institution, or a mental health institution, or a pupil funded
10 under section 53a, is counted in membership in the district or
11 intermediate district approved by the department to operate the
12 program.
13 (e) A pupil enrolled in the Michigan Schools for the Deaf and
14 Blind is counted in membership in the pupil's intermediate district
15 of residence.
16 (f) A pupil enrolled in a career and technical education
17 program supported by a millage levied over an area larger than a
18 single district or in an area vocational-technical education
19 program established under section 690 of the revised school code,
20 MCL 380.690, is counted in membership only in the pupil's district
21 of residence.
22 (g) A pupil enrolled in a public school academy is counted in
23 membership in the public school academy.
24 (h) For the purposes of this section and section 6a, for a
25 cyber school, as that term is defined in section 551 of the revised
26 school code, MCL 380.551, that is in compliance with section 553a
27 of the revised school code, MCL 380.553a, a pupil's participation
28 in the cyber school's educational program is considered regular
29 daily attendance, and for a district or public school academy, a
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

6
1 pupil's participation in a virtual course as that term is defined
2 in section 21f is considered regular daily attendance. Beginning
3 July 1, 2021, this subdivision is subject to section 8c. It is the
4 intent of the legislature that the immediately preceding sentence
5 apply retroactively and is effective July 1, 2021. For the purposes
6 of this subdivision, for a pupil enrolled in a cyber school, all of
7 the following apply with regard to the participation requirement as
8 described in this subdivision:
9 (i) Except as otherwise provided in this subdivision, the pupil
10 shall participate in each scheduled course on pupil membership
11 count day or supplemental count day, as applicable. If the pupil is
12 absent on pupil membership count day or supplemental count day, as
13 applicable, the pupil must attend and participate in class during
14 the next 10 consecutive school days if the absence was unexcused,
15 or during the next 30 calendar days if the absence was excused.
16 (ii) For a pupil who is not learning sequentially, 1 or more of
17 the following must be met on pupil membership count day or
18 supplemental count day, as applicable, for each scheduled course to
19 satisfy the participation requirement under this subdivision:
20 (A) The pupil attended a live lesson from the teacher.
21 (B) The pupil logged into a lesson or lesson activity and the
22 login can be documented.
23 (C) The pupil and teacher engaged in a subject-oriented
24 telephone conversation.
25 (D) There is documentation of an email dialogue between the
26 pupil and teacher.
27 (E) There is documentation of activity or work between the
28 learning coach and pupil.
29 (F) An alternate form of attendance as determined and agreed
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

7
1 upon by the cyber school and the pupil membership auditor was met.
2 (iii) For a pupil using sequential learning, the participation
3 requirement under this subdivision is satisfied if either of the
4 following occurs:
5 (A) Except as otherwise provided in this sub-subparagraph, the
6 pupil and the teacher of record or mentor complete a 2-way
7 interaction for 1 course during the week on which pupil membership
8 count day or supplemental count day, as applicable, occurs, and the
9 3 consecutive weeks following that week. However, if a school break
10 is scheduled during any of the weeks described in this sub-
11 subparagraph that is 4 or more days in length or instruction has
12 been canceled districtwide during any of the weeks described in
13 this sub-subparagraph for 3 or more school days, the district is
14 not required to ensure that the pupil and the teacher of record or
15 mentor completed a 2-way interaction for that week. As used in this
16 sub-subparagraph:
17 (I) "2-way interaction" means the communication that occurs
18 between the teacher of record or mentor and pupil, where 1 party
19 initiates communication and a response from the other party follows
20 that communication. Responses as described in this sub-sub-
21 subparagraph must be to the communication initiated by the teacher
22 of record or mentor, and not some other action taken. This
23 interaction may occur through, but is not limited to, means such as
24 email, telephone, instant messaging, or face-to-face conversation.
25 A parent- or legal-guardian-facilitated 2-way interaction is
26 considered a 2-way interaction if the pupil is in any of grades K
27 to 5 and does not yet possess the skills necessary to participate
28 in 2-way interactions unassisted. The interactions described in
29 this sub-sub-subparagraph must relate to a virtual course on the
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

8
1 pupil's schedule and pertain to course content or progress.
2 (II) "Mentor" means a professional employee of the district
3 who monitors the pupil's progress, ensures the pupil has access to
4 needed technology, is available for assistance, and ensures access
5 to the teacher of record. A mentor may also be the teacher of
6 record if the mentor meets the definition of a teacher of record
7 under this sub-subparagraph and the district is the provider for
8 the course.
9 (III) "Teacher of record" means a teacher to whom all of the
10 following apply:
11 (1) The teacher is responsible for providing instruction,
12 determining instructional methods for each pupil, diagnosing
13 learning needs, assessing pupil learning, prescribing intervention
14 strategies and modifying lessons, reporting outcomes, and
15 evaluating the effects of instruction and support strategies. The
16 teacher of record may coordinate the distribution and assignment of
17 the responsibilities described in this sub-sub-sub-subparagraph
18 with other teachers participating in the instructional process for
19 a course.
20 (2) The teacher is certified for the grade level or is working
21 under a valid substitute permit, authorization, or approval issued
22 by the department.
23 (3) The teacher has a personnel identification code provided
24 by the center.
25 (IV) "Week" means a period that starts on Wednesday and ends
26 the following Tuesday.
27 (B) The pupil completes a combination of 1 or more of the
28 following activities for each scheduled course on pupil membership
29 count day or supplemental count day, as applicable:
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

9
1 (I) Documented attendance in a virtual course where
2 synchronous, live instruction occurred with the teacher.
3 (II) Documented completion of a course assignment.
4 (III) Documented completion of a course lesson or lesson
5 activity.
6 (IV) Documented pupil access to an ongoing lesson, which does
7 not include a login.
8 (V) Documented physical attendance on pupil membership count
9 day or supplemental count day, as applicable, in each scheduled
10 course, if the pupil will attend at least 50% of the instructional
11 time for each scheduled course on-site, face-to-face with the
12 teacher of record. As used in this sub-sub-subparagraph, "teacher
13 of record" means that term as defined in subparagraph (iii)(A).
14 (iv) For purposes of subparagraph (iii), each scheduled course
15 currently being attempted by the pupil, rather than every course on
16 the pupil's schedule for the entire term, is considered a part of
17 each scheduled course for the pupil.

CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

24
1 whose parent or legal guardian has made an official written
2 complaint to law enforcement officials and to school officials of
3 the pupil's district of residence that the pupil has been the
4 victim of a criminal sexual assault or other serious assault, if
5 the official complaint either indicates that the assault occurred
6 at school or that the assault was committed by 1 or more other
7 pupils enrolled in the school the pupil would otherwise attend in
8 the district of residence or by an employee of the district of
9 residence. A person who intentionally makes a false report of a
10 crime to law enforcement officials for the purposes of this
11 subdivision is subject to section 411a of the Michigan penal code,
12 1931 PA 328, MCL 750.411a, which provides criminal penalties for
13 that conduct. As used in this subdivision:
14 (i) "At school" means in a classroom, elsewhere on school
15 premises, on a school bus or other school-related vehicle, or at a
16 school-sponsored activity or event whether or not it is held on
17 school premises.
18 (ii) "Serious assault" means an act that constitutes a felony
19 violation of chapter XI of the Michigan penal code, 1931 PA 328,
20 MCL 750.81 to 750.90h, 750.90g, or that constitutes an assault and
21 infliction of serious or aggravated injury under section 81a of the
22 Michigan penal code, 1931 PA 328, MCL 750.81a.

24 pupil membership count day and before the supplemental count day
25 and who continues to be enrolled on the supplemental count day as a
26 nonresident in the district in which the pupil was enrolled as a
27 resident on the pupil membership count day of the same school year.

1 who meets 1 or more of the following:

13 (7) "Pupil membership count day" of a district or intermediate
14 district means:
15 (a) Except as provided in subdivision (b) or (c), either of
16 the following:
17 (i) The first Wednesday in October each school year.
18 (ii) For a district or building in which school is not in
19 session on the Wednesday described in subparagraph (i) due to
20 conditions not within the control of school authorities, with the
21 approval of the superintendent, the immediately following day on
22 which school is in session in the district or building.
23 (b) Except as otherwise provided in subdivision (c), for a
24 district or intermediate district maintaining school during the
25 entire school year, the following days:
26 (i) Fourth Wednesday in July.
27 (ii) First Wednesday in October.
28 (iii) Second Wednesday in February.
29 (iv) Fourth Wednesday in April.

5 (8) "Pupils in grades K to 12 actually enrolled and in regular
6 daily attendance" means, except as otherwise provided in this
7 section, pupils in grades K to 12 in attendance and receiving
8 instruction in all classes for which they are enrolled on the pupil
9 membership count day or the supplemental count day, as applicable.
10 Except as otherwise provided in this section and subsection, a
11 pupil who is absent from any of the classes in which the pupil is
12 enrolled on the pupil membership count day or supplemental count
13 day and who does not attend each of those classes during the 10
14 consecutive school days immediately following the pupil membership
15 count day or supplemental count day, except for a pupil who has
16 been excused by the district, is not counted as 1.0 full-time
17 equated membership. Except as otherwise provided in this section, a
18 pupil who is excused from attendance on the pupil membership count
19 day or supplemental count day and who fails to attend each of the
20 classes in which the pupil is enrolled within 30 calendar days
21 after the pupil membership count day or supplemental count day is

1 public school academy within 45 days after the pupil membership

6 attended. For purposes of this subsection:

21 substitute permit, authorization, or approval issued by the

26 of pupils in the district, as reported to the center in the form

1 building level. This sub-subparagraph applies only to an eligible

3 year in which it is in the community eligibility program. As used

5 quotient of the number of pupils in an eligible district who are
6 determined to be economically disadvantaged, as reported to the
7 center in a form and manner prescribed by the center not later than
8 the fifth Wednesday after the pupil membership count day in the
9 fiscal year preceding the first fiscal year in which the eligible
10 district is in the community eligibility program, divided by the
11 total number of pupils counted in an eligible district on the pupil
12 membership count day in the fiscal year preceding the first fiscal
13 year in which the eligible district is in the community eligibility
14 program.

16 pupil membership count day of the current fiscal year, the number
17 of membership pupils in the district who are determined to be
18 economically disadvantaged, as reported to the center in the form
19 and manner prescribed by the center not later than the fifth
20 Wednesday after the supplemental count day of the current fiscal
21 year.

7 (ii) Each district or public school academy must be assigned an

9 quotient of the number of economically disadvantaged pupils as

12 school academy in the immediately preceding fiscal year, multiplied

23 (G) As used in this subparagraph, "band adjustment factor"
24 means an amount equal to the difference between the lowest and
25 highest reimbursement bounds for each band, divided by the number
26 of possible opportunity index scores in that band.

8 under the Richard B. Russell national school lunch act, 42 USC 1751

16 understanding English as reported to the center.

19 Sec. 11a. (1) The school aid stabilization fund is created as
20 a separate account within the state school aid fund.
21 (2) The state treasurer may receive money or other assets from
22 any source for deposit into the school aid stabilization fund. The
23 state treasurer shall deposit into the school aid stabilization
24 fund all of the following:
25 (a) Unexpended and unencumbered state school aid fund revenue
26 for a fiscal year that remains in the state school aid fund as of
27 the bookclosing for that fiscal year.
28 (b) Money statutorily dedicated to the school aid
29 stabilization fund.
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

51
1 (c) Money appropriated to the school aid stabilization fund.
2 (3) Money available in the school aid stabilization fund may
3 not be expended without a specific appropriation from the school
4 aid stabilization fund. Money in the school aid stabilization fund
5 must be expended only for purposes for which state school aid fund
6 money may be expended.
7 (4) The state treasurer shall direct the investment of the
8 school aid stabilization fund. The state treasurer shall credit to
9 the school aid stabilization fund interest and earnings from fund
10 investments.
11 (5) Money in the school aid stabilization fund at the close of
12 a fiscal year remains in the school aid stabilization fund and does
13 not lapse to the unreserved school aid fund balance or the general
14 fund.
15 (6) If the maximum amount appropriated under section 11 from
16 the state school aid fund for a fiscal year exceeds the amount
17 available for expenditure from the state school aid fund for that
18 fiscal year, there is appropriated from the school aid
19 stabilization fund to the state school aid fund an amount equal to
20 the projected shortfall as determined by the department of
21 treasury, but not to exceed available money in the school aid
22 stabilization fund. If the money in the school aid stabilization
23 fund is insufficient to fully fund an amount equal to the projected
24 shortfall, the state budget director shall notify the legislature
25 as required under section 296(2) and state payments in an amount
26 equal to the remainder of the projected shortfall must be prorated
27 in the manner provided under section 296(3).

2 transportation payment, from the proceeds of a loan to the district

4 141.942, or from the proceeds of millage levied or pledged under
5 section 1211 of the revised school code, MCL 380.1211. State aid
6 overpayments made in special education or special education
7 transportation payments may be recovered from subsequent special
8 education or special education transportation payments, from the
9 proceeds of a loan to the district under the emergency municipal
10 loan act, 1980 PA 243, MCL 141.931 to 141.942, or from the proceeds
11 of millage levied or pledged under section 1211 of the revised
12 school code, MCL 380.1211.
13 (2) If the result of an audit conducted by or for the
14 department affects the current fiscal year membership, the
15 department shall adjust affected payments in the current fiscal
16 year. A deduction due to an adjustment made as a result of an audit
17 conducted by or for the department, or as a result of information
18 obtained by the department from the district, an intermediate
19 district, the department of treasury, or the office of auditor
20 general, must be deducted from the district's apportionments when
21 the adjustment is finalized. At the request of the district and
22 upon the district presenting evidence satisfactory to the
23 department of the hardship, the department may grant up to an
24 additional 4 years for the adjustment and may advance payments to
25 the district otherwise authorized under this article if the
26 district would otherwise experience a significant hardship in
27 satisfying its financial obligations. However, a district that
28 presented satisfactory evidence of hardship and was undergoing an
29 extended adjustment during 2018-2019 may continue to use the period
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

1 of extended adjustment as originally granted by the department.
2 (3) If, based on an audit by the department or the
3 department's designee or because of new or updated information
4 received by the department, the department determines that the
5 amount paid to a district or intermediate district under this
6 article for the current fiscal year or a prior fiscal year was
7 incorrect, the department shall make the appropriate deduction or
8 payment in the district's or intermediate district's allocation in
9 the next apportionment after the adjustment is finalized. The
10 department shall calculate the deduction or payment according to
11 the law in effect in the fiscal year in which the incorrect amount

19 (3) Except as otherwise provided in this section, the
20 department shall calculate the amount of a district's foundation
21 allowance as follows, using in all calculations the total amount of
22 the district's foundation allowance as calculated before any
23 proration:
24 (a) For a district that had a foundation allowance for the
25 immediately preceding fiscal year that was equal to the target
26 foundation allowance for the immediately preceding fiscal year, the
27 district receives a foundation allowance in an amount equal to the
28 target foundation allowance described in subsection (1) for the
29 current fiscal year.
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

76
1 (b) For a district that had a foundation allowance for the
2 immediately preceding fiscal year that was greater than the target
3 foundation allowance for the immediately preceding fiscal year, the
4 district's foundation allowance is an amount equal to the lesser of
5 (the sum of the district's foundation allowance for the immediately
6 preceding fiscal year plus any per pupil amount calculated under
7 section 20m(2) in the immediately preceding fiscal year plus the
8 increase in the target foundation allowance for the current fiscal
9 year, as compared to the immediately preceding fiscal year) or (the
10 product of the district's foundation allowance for the immediately
11 preceding fiscal year times the percentage increase in the United
12 States Consumer Price Index in the calendar year ending in the
13 immediately preceding fiscal year as reported by the May revenue
14 estimating conference conducted under section 367b of the
15 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1367b).
16 (c) For a district that had a foundation allowance in the
17 immediately preceding fiscal year that was less than the target
18 foundation allowance in effect for that fiscal year, the district's
19 foundation allowance is an amount equal to the lesser of (the sum
20 of district's foundation allowance for the immediately preceding
21 fiscal year plus any per pupil amount calculated under section
22 20m(2) in the immediately preceding fiscal year plus the increase
23 in the target foundation allowance for the current fiscal year, as
24 compared to the immediately preceding fiscal year) or (the product
25 of the district's foundation allowance for the immediately
26 preceding fiscal year times the percentage increase in the United
27 States Consumer Price Index in the calendar year ending in the
28 immediately preceding fiscal year as reported by the May revenue
29 estimating conference conducted under section 367b of the
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

77
1 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1367b).

3 a whole dollar amount, the department shall round the district's
4 foundation allowance up to the nearest whole dollar.

6 portion of a district's foundation allowance is an amount equal to

16 (11) To assist the legislature in determining the target
17 foundation allowance for the subsequent fiscal year, each revenue
18 estimating conference conducted under section 367b of the
19 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1367b, must
20 calculate a pupil membership factor, a revenue adjustment factor,
21 and an index as follows:
22 (a) The pupil membership factor is computed by dividing the
23 estimated membership in the school year ending in the current
24 fiscal year, excluding intermediate district membership, by the
25 estimated membership for the school year ending in the subsequent
26 fiscal year, excluding intermediate district membership. If a
27 consensus membership factor is not determined at the revenue
28 estimating conference, the principals of the revenue estimating
29 conference shall report their estimates to the house and senate
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

81
1 subcommittees responsible for school aid appropriations not later
2 than 7 days after the conclusion of the revenue conference.
3 (b) The revenue adjustment factor is computed by dividing the
4 sum of the estimated total state school aid fund revenue for the
5 subsequent fiscal year plus the estimated total state school aid
6 fund revenue for the current fiscal year, adjusted for any change
7 in the rate or base of a tax the proceeds of which are deposited in
8 that fund and excluding money transferred into that fund from the
9 countercyclical budget and economic stabilization fund under the
10 management and budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1101 to 18.1594, by
11 the sum of the estimated total school aid fund revenue for the
12 current fiscal year plus the estimated total state school aid fund
13 revenue for the immediately preceding fiscal year, adjusted for any
14 change in the rate or base of a tax the proceeds of which are
15 deposited in that fund. If a consensus revenue factor is not
16 determined at the revenue estimating conference, the principals of
17 the revenue estimating conference shall report their estimates to
18 the house and senate subcommittees responsible for school aid
19 appropriations not later than 7 days after the conclusion of the
20 revenue conference.
21 (c) The index is calculated by multiplying the pupil
22 membership factor by the revenue adjustment factor. If a consensus
23 index is not determined at the revenue estimating conference, the
24 principals of the revenue estimating conference shall report their
25 estimates to the house and senate subcommittees responsible for
26 state school aid appropriations not later than 7 days after the
27 conclusion of the revenue conference.
