5 Sec. 21f. (1) A primary district shall enroll an eligible
6 pupil in virtual courses in accordance with the provisions of this
7 section. A primary district shall not offer a virtual course to an
8 eligible pupil unless the virtual course is published in the
9 primary district's catalog of board-approved courses or in the
10 statewide catalog of virtual courses maintained by the Michigan
11 Virtual University pursuant to section 98. The primary district
12 shall also provide on its publicly accessible website a link to the
13 statewide catalog of virtual courses maintained by the Michigan
14 Virtual University. Unless the pupil is at least age 18 or is an
15 emancipated minor, a pupil must not be enrolled in a course that
16 meets virtually for more than 6 days in a school year without the
17 consent of the pupil's parent or legal guardian.
18 (2) Subject to subsection (3), a primary district shall enroll
19 an eligible pupil in up to 2 virtual courses as requested by the
20 pupil during an academic term, semester, or trimester.
21 (3) A pupil may be enrolled in more than 2 virtual courses in
22 a specific academic term, semester, or trimester if both of the
23 following conditions are met:
24 (a) The primary district has determined that it is in the best
25 interest of the pupil.
26 (b) The pupil agrees with the recommendation of the primary
27 district.
28 (4) If the number of applicants eligible for acceptance in a
29 virtual course does not exceed the capacity of the provider to
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

88
1 provide the virtual course, the provider shall accept for
2 enrollment all of the applicants eligible for acceptance. If the
3 number of applicants exceeds the provider's capacity to provide the
4 virtual course, the provider shall use a random draw system,
5 subject to the need to abide by state and federal
6 antidiscrimination laws and court orders. A primary district that
7 is also a provider shall determine whether or not it has the
8 capacity to accept applications for enrollment from nonresident
9 applicants in virtual courses and may use that limit as the reason
10 for refusal to enroll a nonresident applicant.
11 (5) A primary district may not establish additional
12 requirements beyond those specified in this subsection that would
13 prohibit a pupil from taking a virtual course. A pupil's primary
14 district may deny the pupil enrollment in a virtual course if any
15 of the following apply, as determined by the district:
16 (a) The pupil is enrolled in any of grades K to 5.
17 (b) The pupil has previously gained the credits that would be
18 provided from the completion of the virtual course.
19 (c) The virtual course is not capable of generating academic
20 credit.
21 (d) The virtual course is inconsistent with the remaining
22 graduation requirements or career interests of the pupil.
23 (e) The pupil has not completed the prerequisite coursework
24 for the requested virtual course or has not demonstrated
25 proficiency in the prerequisite course content.
26 (f) The pupil has failed a previous virtual course in the same
27 subject during the 2 most recent academic years.
28 (g) The virtual course is of insufficient quality or rigor. A
29 primary district that denies a pupil enrollment request for this
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

89
1 reason shall enroll the pupil in a virtual course in the same or a
2 similar subject that the primary district determines is of
3 acceptable rigor and quality.
4 (h) The cost of the virtual course exceeds the amount
5 identified in subsection (10), unless the pupil or the pupil's
6 parent or legal guardian agrees to pay the cost that exceeds this
7 amount.
8 (i) The request for a virtual course enrollment did not occur
9 within the same timelines established by the primary district for
10 enrollment and schedule changes for regular courses.
11 (j) The request for a virtual course enrollment was not made
12 in the academic term, semester, trimester, or summer preceding the
13 enrollment. This subdivision does not apply to a request made by a
14 pupil who is newly enrolled in the primary district.
15 (6) If a pupil is denied enrollment in a virtual course by the
16 pupil's primary district, the primary district shall provide
17 written notification to the pupil of the denial, the reason or
18 reasons for the denial under subsection (5), and a description of
19 the appeal process. The pupil may appeal the denial by submitting a
20 letter to the superintendent of the intermediate district in which
21 the pupil's primary district is located. The letter of appeal must
22 include the reason provided by the primary district for not
23 enrolling the pupil and the reason why the pupil is claiming that
24 the enrollment should be approved. The intermediate district
25 superintendent or designee shall respond to the appeal within 5
26 days after it is received. If the intermediate district
27 superintendent or designee determines that the denial of enrollment
28 does not meet 1 or more of the reasons specified in subsection (5),
29 the primary district shall enroll the pupil in the virtual course.
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

90
1 (7) To provide a virtual course to an eligible pupil under
2 this section, a provider must do all of the following:
3 (a) Ensure that the virtual course has been published in the
4 pupil's primary district's catalog of board-approved courses or
5 published in the statewide catalog of virtual courses maintained by
6 the Michigan Virtual University.
7 (b) Assign to each pupil a teacher of record and provide the
8 primary district with the personnel identification code assigned by
9 the center for the teacher of record. If the provider is a
10 community college, the virtual course must be taught by an
11 instructor employed by or contracted through the providing
12 community college.
13 (c) Offer the virtual course on an open entry and exit method,
14 or aligned to a semester, trimester, or accelerated academic term
15 format.
16 (d) If the virtual course is offered to eligible pupils in
17 more than 1 district, the following additional requirements must
18 also be met:
19 (i) Provide the Michigan Virtual University with a course
20 syllabus that meets the definition under subsection (14)(g) in a
21 form and manner prescribed by the Michigan Virtual University for
22 inclusion in a statewide catalog of virtual courses.
23 (ii) Not later than October 1 of each fiscal year, provide the
24 Michigan Virtual University with an aggregated count of enrollments
25 for each virtual course the provider delivered to pupils under this
26 section during the immediately preceding school year, and the
27 number of enrollments in which the pupil earned 60% or more of the
28 total course points for each virtual course.
29 (8) To provide a virtual course under this section, a
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

91
1 community college shall ensure that each virtual course it provides
2 under this section generates postsecondary credit.
3 (9) For any virtual course a pupil enrolls in under this
4 section, the pupil's primary district must assign to the pupil a
5 mentor and shall supply the provider with the mentor's contact
6 information.
7 (10) For a pupil enrolled in 1 or more virtual courses, the
8 primary district shall use foundation allowance or per-pupil funds
9 calculated under section 20 to pay for the expenses associated with
10 the virtual course or courses. A primary district is not required
11 to pay toward the cost of a virtual course an amount that exceeds
12 6.67% of the target foundation allowance for the current fiscal
13 year as calculated under section 20.
14 (11) A virtual learning pupil has the same rights and access
15 to technology in the pupil's primary district's school facilities

17 department shall establish standards for hardware, software, and
18 internet access for pupils who are enrolled in more than 2 virtual
19 courses under this section in an academic term, semester, or
20 trimester taken at a location other than a school facility.
21 (12) If a pupil successfully completes a virtual course, as
22 determined by the pupil's primary district, the pupil's primary
23 district shall grant appropriate academic credit for completion of
24 the course and shall count that credit toward completion of
25 graduation and subject area requirements. A pupil's school record
26 and transcript must identify the virtual course title as it appears
27 in the virtual course syllabus.

29 must not result in a pupil being counted as more than 1.0 full-time
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

1 equivalent pupils under this article. The minimum requirements to

6 days before the amendment becomes effective.
7 (14) Subject to the requirements of this subsection, a
8 district may provide virtual instruction under this subsection for
9 not more than 6 days in a school year. If a district plans to
10 provide instruction under this subsection to pupils for more than 6
11 days during a school year, the district's plan must be approved by
12 the board of the district, and the district must provide notice of
13 the plan to impacted pupils and their parents or legal guardians

16 Days of instruction under this subsection may be used only for the
17 following purposes, as defined by the department:
18 (a) A maximum of 3 days may be designated as testing days,

20 instruction to participate in state or federally mandated
21 assessments. Days of virtual instruction provided under this
22 subdivision are limited to buildings and grade levels impacted by
23 assessments.
24 (b) A maximum of 3 days of virtual instruction may be provided
25 due to conditions not within control of the district, including,
26 but not limited to, closures due to snow, ice, or other extreme

5 (16) (14) As used in this section:
6 (a) "Instructor" means an individual who is employed by or
7 contracted through a community college.
8 (b) "Mentor" means a professional employee of the primary
9 district who monitors the pupil's progress, ensures the pupil has
10 access to needed technology, is available for assistance, and
11 ensures access to the teacher of record. A mentor may also serve as
12 the teacher of record if the primary district is the provider for
13 the virtual course and the mentor meets the requirements under
14 subdivision (e).
15 (c) "Primary district" means the district that enrolls the
16 pupil and reports the pupil for pupil membership purposes.
17 (d) "Provider" means the district, intermediate district,
18 community college, or other third-party vendor that the primary
19 district pays to provide the virtual course or the Michigan Virtual
20 University if it is providing the virtual course.
21 (e) "Teacher of record" means a teacher who meets all of the
22 following:
23 (i) Is appropriately placed under a valid Michigan teaching
24 certificate or a teaching permit, authorization, or approval issued
25 by the department. As used in this subparagraph, "appropriately
26 placed" means holding a valid Michigan educator credential with the
27 required grade range and discipline or subject area for the
28 assignment, as defined by the superintendent of public instruction.
29 (ii) Is responsible for providing instruction, determining
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

94
1 instructional methods for each pupil, diagnosing learning needs,
2 assessing pupil learning, prescribing intervention strategies and
3 modifying lessons, reporting outcomes, and evaluating the effects
4 of instruction and support strategies.
5 (iii) Has a personnel identification code provided by the
6 center.
7 (iv) If the provider is a community college, is an instructor
8 employed by or contracted through the providing community college.
9 (f) "Virtual course" means a course of study that is capable
10 of generating a credit or a grade and that is provided in an
11 interactive learning environment where any portion of the
12 curriculum is delivered using the internet and in which pupils may
13 be separated from their instructor or teacher of record by time or
14 location, or both.
15 (g) "Virtual course syllabus" means a document that includes
16 all of the following:
17 (i) An alignment document detailing how the course meets
18 applicable state standards or, if the state does not have state
19 standards, nationally recognized standards.
20 (ii) The virtual course content outline.
21 (iii) The virtual course required assessments.
22 (iv) The virtual course prerequisites.
23 (v) Expectations for actual instructor or teacher of record
24 contact time with the virtual learning pupil and other
25 communications between a pupil and the instructor or teacher of
26 record.
27 (vi) Academic support available to the virtual learning pupil.
28 (vii) The virtual course learning outcomes and objectives.
29 (viii) The name of the institution or organization providing the
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

95
1 virtual content.
2 (ix) The name of the institution or organization providing the
3 instructor or teacher of record.
4 (x) The course titles assigned by the provider and the course
5 titles and course codes from the National Center for Education
6 Statistics (NCES) school codes for the exchange of data (SCED).
7 (xi) The number of eligible pupils that will be accepted by the
8 provider in the virtual course. A primary district that is also the
9 provider may limit the enrollment to those pupils enrolled in the
10 primary district.
11 (xii) The results of the virtual course quality review using
12 the guidelines and model review process published by the Michigan
13 Virtual University.
14 (h) "Virtual learning pupil" means a pupil enrolled in 1 or
15 more virtual courses.
16 Sec. 21h. (1) From the state school aid pupil support reserve
17 fund money appropriated in section 11, there is allocated
18 $6,137,400.00 for 2025-2026 2026-2027 for assisting districts
19 assigned by the superintendent to participate in a partnership
20 district agreement to improve student achievement and district
21 financial stability. It is the intent of the legislature that the
22 appropriation in this section will be funded with state school aid
23 pupil support reserve fund money through 2027-2028. The
24 superintendent shall identify any conditions that may be
25 contributing to low academic performance within a district being
26 considered for assignment to a partnership district agreement. The
27 purpose of the partnership district agreement is to identify
28 district needs, develop intervention plans, and partner with
29 public, private, and nonprofit organizations to coordinate
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

96
1 resources and improve student achievement. Assignment of a district
2 to a partnership district agreement is made by the superintendent.
3 (2) A district described in subsection (1) is eligible for
4 funding under this section if the district includes at least 1
5 school that has been identified as low performing under the
6 approved federal accountability system. A district described in
7 this subsection must do all of the following to be eligible for
8 funding under this section:
9 (a) Within 90 days of assignment to the partnership district
10 agreement described in this section, complete a comprehensive needs
11 assessment or evaluation in collaboration with an intermediate
12 district, community members, education organizations, and
13 postsecondary institutions, as applicable, that is approved by the
14 superintendent. The comprehensive needs assessment or evaluation
15 must include at least all of the following:
16 (i) A review of the district's implementation and utilization
17 of a multi-tiered system of supports to ensure that it is used to
18 appropriately inform instruction.
19 (ii) A review of the district and school building leadership
20 and educator capacity to substantially improve student outcomes.
21 (iii) A review of classroom, instructional, and operational
22 practices and curriculum to ensure alignment with research-based
23 instructional practices and state curriculum standards.
24 (b) Develop a district continuous improvement plan that has
25 been approved by the superintendent and that addresses the needs
26 identified in the comprehensive needs assessment or evaluation
27 completed under subdivision (a). The district continuous
28 improvement plan must include at least all of the following:
29 (i) Specific actions that will be taken by the district and
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

97
1 each of its partners to improve student achievement.
2 (ii) Specific measurable benchmarks that will be met within 18
3 months to improve student achievement and identification of
4 expected student achievement outcomes to be attained within 3 years
5 after assignment to the partnership.
6 (iii) Measurable benchmarks that put pupils on track to meet or
7 exceed grade level proficiency, increase high school graduation
8 rates, reduce class sizes in grades K to 3, and improve attendance
9 rates.
10 (c) Provide access to training for district leadership,
11 including, but not limited to, the superintendent or chief
12 administrator and school board or board of directors members, on
13 areas of education fiscal and policy matters. The department may
14 require training for district leadership and all board members
15 under this subdivision at a rate and frequency needed to support
16 measurable academic outcomes for the district.
17 (3) Upon approval of the district continuous improvement plan
18 developed under subsection (2), the department shall assign a team
19 of individuals with expertise in comprehensive school and district
20 reform to partner with the district, the intermediate district,
21 community organizations, education organizations, and postsecondary
22 institutions identified in the academic and financial operating or
23 intervention district continuous improvement plan to review the
24 district's use of existing financial resources to ensure that those
25 resources are being used as efficiently and effectively as possible
26 to improve student academic achievement and to ensure district
27 financial stability. The superintendent of public instruction may
28 waive burdensome administrative rules for a partnership district
29 for the duration of the partnership district agreement.

21 380.1211, as certified by the department of treasury for the 1994
22 tax year. For a receiving district, if school operating taxes are

24 (b) The balance of the funding under subsection (4) is
25 allocated as follows:
26 (i) For districts with at least 8.0 but fewer than 9.0 pupils
27 per square mile, as determined by the department, the allocation is
28 an amount per pupil equal to 75% of the per-pupil amount allocated
29 to districts under subdivision (a).

1 (ii) For districts with at least 9.0 but fewer than 10.0 pupils

120
1 (b) Utilizing the infrastructure to put in place commonly

6 network.

11 the cost of tuition at an educator preparation program that is an
12 alternative certification provider is the cost of tuition plus
13 other required fees, as determined by the department of lifelong
14 education, advancement, and potential.
15 (5) Awards under this section must be distributed to eligible
16 educator preparation programs on behalf of an eligible recipient on
17 a timeline determined by the department of lifelong education,
18 advancement, and potential.
19 (6) Pending available funds, applicants may renew their award
20 for up to 3 years, or until program completion, whichever comes
21 first.
22 (7) To be an eligible recipient of fellowship funding under
23 this section, an individual must pledge to work as a certified
24 teacher in a public school or a qualifying public preschool program
25 in this state and must meet 1 of the following work requirements:
26 (a) For a recipient of funding under this section who received
27 an award for 1 academic year, 3 years of work as a certified
28 teacher in a public school or a qualifying public preschool program
29 in this state.

134
1 (b) For a recipient of funding under this section who received
2 an award for 2 academic years, 4 years of work as a certified

4 in this state.
5 (c) For a recipient of funding under this section who received
6 an award for 3 academic years, 5 years of work as a certified
7 teacher in a public school or a qualifying public preschool program
8 in this state.
9 (d) For a recipient working in a critical needs district, 3
10 years of work as a certified teacher. As used in this subdivision,
11 "critical needs district" means a district with a median household
12 income in the lowest quartile in each prosperity region, as
13 determined by the department.

16 entity must participate in the National School Lunch Program and
17 School Breakfast Program and must do all of the following:

23 by the department.

25 and reimbursable lunches by operating under the CEP if the

1 single school, a group or groups of schools, or all schools in the

5 school breakfast and lunch programs, as determined by the
6 department.

8 require parents or guardians to fill out relevant family income
9 information, in a manner prescribed by the department, for the
10 purposes of determining student eligibility for federal free or
11 reduced cost meal reimbursement rates and CEP eligibility
12 determinations.

14 reimbursable meals, as determined by the department.

25 (4) Each district or public school academy must be assigned an
26 opportunity index score each fiscal year, the value of which is the
27 quotient of the number of economically disadvantaged pupils as
28 determined under subsection (3) for the district or public school
29 academy and the total number of pupils in the district or public
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

160
1 school academy in the immediately preceding fiscal year, multiplied
2 by 100 and rounded up to the nearest whole number. Each district or
3 public school academy must be assigned an opportunity index band as
4 follows:
5 (a) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
6 index score greater than or equal to 0 but less than 20 must be
7 assigned to band 1 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
8 (3) at a rate of at least 35.0% and less than 36.0%. The
9 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
10 to the district's opportunity index score minus 1, multiplied by
11 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
12 35.0%.
13 (b) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
14 index score greater than or equal to 20 but less than 44 must be
15 assigned to band 2 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
16 (3) at a rate of at least 36.0% and less than 37.5%. The
17 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
18 to the district's opportunity index score minus 20, multiplied by
19 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
20 36.0%.
21 (c) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
22 index score greater than or equal to 44 but less than 59 must be
23 assigned to band 3 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
24 (3) at a rate of at least 37.5% and less than 39.0%. The
25 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
26 to the district's opportunity index score minus 44, multiplied by
27 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
28 37.5%.
29 (d) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

161
1 index score greater than or equal to 59 but less than 73 must be
2 assigned to band 4 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
3 (3) at a rate of at least 39.0% and less than 42.0%. The
4 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
5 to the district's opportunity index score minus 59, multiplied by
6 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
7 39.0%.
8 (e) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
9 index score greater than or equal to 73 but less than 85 must be
10 assigned to band 5 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection
11 (3) at a rate of at least 42.0% and less than 47.0%. The
12 reimbursement rate under this subdivision must be an amount equal
13 to the district's opportunity index score minus 73, multiplied by
14 the band adjustment factor applicable to this subdivision, plus
15 42.0%.
16 (f) A district or public school academy with an opportunity
17 index score greater than or equal to 85 must be assigned to band 6
18 and shall receive reimbursement under subsection (3) at a rate of
19 47.0%.
20 (g) As used in this subsection, "band adjustment factor" means
21 an amount equal to the difference between the lowest and highest
22 reimbursement bounds for each band, divided by the number of
23 possible opportunity index scores in that band.

22 graduation and dropout report.

1 assessments.
2 (b) "Economically disadvantaged" means a pupil who has been
3 determined eligible for free or reduced-price meals as determined
4 under the Richard B. Russell national school lunch act, 42 USC 1751
5 to 1769j; who is in a household receiving supplemental nutrition
6 assistance program or temporary assistance for needy families
7 assistance; or who is homeless, migrant, or in foster care, as
8 reported to the center.
9 (c) "English language learner" means limited English
10 proficient pupils who speak a language other than English as their
11 primary language and have difficulty speaking, reading, writing, or
12 understanding English as reported to the center.

1 (19) (22) A recipient of funds under this section shall report
2 to the center in a form and manner prescribed by the center the
3 information necessary to derive the number of children
4 participating in the program, the number of eligible children not
5 participating in the program and on a waitlist, and the total
6 number of children participating in the program by various
7 demographic groups and eligibility factors necessary to analyze
8 equitable and priority access to services for the purposes of
9 subsection (3).
10 (20) (23) As used in this section:
11 (a) "Child care center" means that term as defined in section
12 1 of 1973 PA 116, MCL 722.111.
13 (b) "Eligible great start readiness program options" means a
14 program option that operates on a school-day, part-day, or extended
15 schedule length, as determined by the department of lifelong
16 education, advancement, and potential. The department of lifelong
17 education, advancement, and potential must maintain and publish on
18 its website requirements for each eligible schedule length,
19 including the minimum day length, the minimum number of days per
20 week, and the minimum number of weeks per year. These programs may
21 be blended with Head Start programs, if allowable by federal rules
22 and regulations.
23 (c) "Federal poverty guidelines" means the guidelines
24 published annually in the Federal Register by the United States
25 Department of Health and Human Services under its authority to
26 revise the poverty line under 42 USC 9902.
27 (d) "GSRP extended program" means a program that operates for
28 at least the same length of day as a district's first grade program
29 for a minimum of 5 days per week, 36 weeks per year.
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

213
1 (e) "GSRP/Head Start extended blended program" means a program
2 funded under this section and a Head Start program that are
3 combined for an extended program.
4 (f) "GSRP/Head Start school-day blended program" means a part-
5 day program funded under this section and a Head Start program,
6 which are combined for a school-day program.
7 (g) "Licensed child care center" means a child care center
8 that has been issued a license under 1973 PA 116, MCL 722.111 to
9 722.128, to operate a child care center.
10 (h) "Part-day program" means a program that operates at least
11 4 days per week, 30 weeks per year, for at least 3 hours of
12 teacher-child contact time per day but for fewer hours of teacher-
13 child contact time per day than a school-day program.
14 (i) "School-day program" means a program that operates for at
15 least the same length of day as a district's first grade program
16 for a minimum of 4 days per week, 30 weeks per year. A classroom
17 that offers a school-day program must enroll all children for the
18 school day to be considered a school-day program.

4 Sec. 32p. (1) From the state school aid fund money

9 (2) From the state school aid fund money allocated in
10 subsection (1), there is allocated an amount not to exceed
11 $15,900,000.00 to intermediate districts for the purpose of
12 coordinating early childhood services to support the goals and
13 outcomes under subsections (3), (5), (7), and (8) and to provide
14 supports for early childhood programs for children prenatal through
15 age 5. The funding provided to each intermediate district under
16 this section is determined by the distribution formula established
17 by the department of lifelong education, advancement, and potential
18 for the former Great Start collaboratives. To receive funding under
19 this section, each intermediate district must provide an
20 application to the department of lifelong education, advancement,
21 and potential, by a date determined by the department of lifelong
22 education, advancement, and potential, indicating the strategies
23 planned to be provided and how the strategies align with the Help
24 Me Grow National Fidelity Assessment.
25 (3) Each intermediate district or consortium of intermediate
26 districts that receives funding under this section shall convene a
27 Help Me Grow collaborative that includes an active partnership with
28 families and multiple community-based organizations. Each
29 intermediate district is required to employ at least a 0.8 FTE Help
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

232
1 Me Grow lead responsible for local system building activities and
2 at least a 0.5 FTE care coordinator for each intermediate district
3 region. The goal of each Help Me Grow collaborative is to ensure
4 the coordination and expansion of local early childhood systems and
5 programs that achieve the following outcomes:
6 (a) Improve family access, leadership, and engagement.
7 (b) Enhance child development, learning, and well-being.
8 (c) Create strong, efficient community systems.
9 (4) The responsibilities of the intermediate district Help Me
10 Grow lead described in subsection (3) include at least the
11 following:
12 (a) Facilitating local Help Me Grow collaboratives and
13 demonstrating growth toward fidelity to the Help Me Grow model by
14 implementing the following 4 core components of the model and its
15 key activities as indicated by the Help Me Grow National Fidelity
16 Assessment:
17 (i) Centralized access point.
18 (ii) Family and community outreach.
19 (iii) Child health provider outreach.
20 (iv) Data collection and analysis.
21 (b) Ensuring that local early childhood initiatives are data
22 driven and reflect the needs of the community, including literacy,
23 home visiting, and other early childhood programs.
24 (c) Completing the annual Help Me Grow National Fidelity
25 Assessment and tracking relevant data, including referrals,
26 systems-building activities, outreach efforts, developmental
27 screenings, and barriers, to demonstrate progress and inform
28 continuous improvement.
29 (d) Providing universal access to developmental screening for
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

233
1 children birth through age 5.
2 (e) Ensuring the local intermediate district has a dedicated
3 care coordinator to support families in the community, either by
4 hiring locally, collaborating amongst intermediate districts, or
5 contracting with Help Me Grow Michigan. The care coordinator shall
6 have sufficient time dedicated to provide all of the following:
7 (i) Regular support of a public telephone line during work
8 hours to answer family questions.
9 (ii) Follow-up on universal developmental screenings and
10 referrals to appropriate early childhood programs and services.
11 (iii) Data tracking to ensure referrals from Help Me Grow
12 successfully connect to early childhood services.
13 (iv) Coordinated cross-sector supports to ensure that families
14 are seamlessly connected to the full scope of services within the
15 intermediate district region.

1 has done all of the following:

5 instruction and that uses intervention delivered to all pupils in

28 (6) (13) As used in this section:
29 (a) "Dyslexia" means both of the following:
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

254
1 (i) A specific learning disorder that is neurobiological in
2 origin and characterized by difficulties with accurate or fluent
3 word recognition and by poor spelling and decoding abilities that
4 typically result from a deficit in the phonological component of
5 language that is often unexpected in relation to other cognitive
6 abilities and the provision of effective classroom instruction.
7 (ii) A specific learning disorder that may include secondary
8 consequences, such as problems in reading comprehension and a
9 reduced reading experience that can impede the growth of vocabulary
10 and background knowledge and lead to social, emotional, and
11 behavioral difficulties.
12 (b) "Evidence-based" means an activity, program, process,
13 service, strategy, or intervention that demonstrates statistically
14 significant effects on improving pupil outcomes or other relevant
15 outcomes and that meets at least both of the following:
16 (i) At least 1 of the following:
17 (A) Is based on strong evidence from at least 1 well-designed
18 and well-implemented experimental study.
19 (B) Is based on moderate evidence from at least 1 well-
20 designed and well-implemented quasi-experimental study.
21 (C) Is based on promising evidence from at least 1 well-
22 designed and well-implemented correlational study with statistical
23 controls for selection bias.
24 (D) Demonstrates a rationale based on high-quality research
25 findings or positive evaluation that the activity, program,
26 process, service, strategy, or intervention is likely to improve
27 pupil outcomes or other relevant outcomes.
28 (ii) Includes ongoing efforts to examine the effects of the
29 activity, program, process, service, strategy, or intervention.
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

255
1 (c) "Explicit" means direct and deliberate instruction through
2 continuous pupil-teacher interaction that includes teacher
3 modeling, guided practice, and independent practice.
4 (d) "Fluency" means the ability to read with speed, accuracy,
5 and proper expression.
6 (e) "Multi-tiered system of supports" means a comprehensive
7 framework that includes 3 distinct tiers of instructional support
8 and is composed of a collection of evidence-based strategies
9 designed to meet the individual needs and assets of a whole pupil
10 at all achievement levels.
11 (f) "Phonemic awareness" means the conscious awareness of all
12 of the following:
13 (i) Individual speech sounds, including, but not limited to,
14 consonants and vowels, in spoken syllables.
15 (ii) The ability to consciously manipulate through, including,
16 but not limited to, matching, blending, segmenting, deleting, or
17 substituting, individual speech sounds described in subparagraph
18 (i).
19 (iii) All levels of the speech sound system, including, but not
20 limited to, word boundaries, rhyme recognition, stress patterns,
21 syllables, onset-rime units, and phonemes.
22 (g) "Phonological" means relating to the system of contrastive
23 relationships among the speech sounds that constitute the
24 fundamental components of a language.
25 (h) "Progress monitoring" means the assessing of students'
26 academic performance, quantifying students' rates of improvement or
27 progress toward goals, and determining how students are responding
28 to instruction.
29 (i) "Rapid automatized naming (RAN)" means a task that
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

256
1 measures how quickly individuals can name objects; pictures;
2 colors; or symbols, including letters and digits, aloud, which can
3 predict later reading abilities for preliterate children.
4 Sec. 35e. (1) From the state school aid fund money
5 appropriated in section 11, there is allocated for 2025-2026 only

8 (2) Wayne State University shall convene a committee to

10 consist of 1 member from each of the following organizations:
11 (a) The department.
12 (b) The Library of Michigan.

14 (d) The Michigan senate.
15 (e) The Michigan house of representatives.
16 (f) The Michigan Education Association.
17 (g) The Michigan chapter of the American Federation of
18 Teachers.
19 (h) The Michigan Association of Superintendents and
20 Administrators.
21 (i) A Michigan research university.
22 (3) The committee described in subsection (2) shall conduct a
23 study to investigate at least all of the following issues:
24 (a) How many schools in each district have a school library.
25 (b) What hours school libraries are open each week for
26 students and faculty to use.
27 (c) What full-time equivalency of certified school librarians
28 are employed at each building.
29 (d) The ratio of students per certified school librarian.
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

257
1 (e) What full-time equivalency of paraprofessional or other
2 staff are employed in the school library and the credentialing of
3 these staff, if any.
4 (f) How school libraries are scheduled.
5 (g) How many hours each week school librarians provide direct
6 library-related instruction to students.
7 (h) The technology available for students to access library
8 resources and lessons.
9 (i) The size and age of the collection in each school library,
10 and the extent of digital materials available for students to
11 access.
12 (j) Current funding per student for school library materials.

14 the fulfillment of its mission to determine the status of school
15 library programs in this state.

25 at large.

25 (2) The department shall continue the committee for literacy
26 achievement that includes representatives of higher education and
27 early childhood literacy educators who have expertise in literacy
28 instruction and research. The committee shall exist until at least
29 September 2029. The department shall appoint the members of the
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

259
1 committee for literacy achievement. The department shall develop a
2 process to identify and assemble experts for the committee. The
3 primary focus of the committee is to recommend tools and strategies
4 with the goal of increasing student academic outcomes in the area
5 of literacy. The committee is encouraged to collaborate with the
6 Michigan Education Research Institute or other entities with
7 similar expertise on the subject of literacy. The department shall
8 determine the size and benchmarks of the committee. The committee
9 shall do all of the following:
10 (a) Critically evaluate early literacy series and materials.
11 The committee shall evaluate with research-based outcomes, using
12 the committee's expertise in the field of literacy. The committee
13 shall assign grade tiers to the series based on the series'
14 likelihood to increase student literacy outcomes. Priority must be
15 given to series that already have a demonstrated history of
16 increasing student outcomes. The committee may perform an
17 evaluation under this subdivision based on submissions from a
18 vendor, but must independently verify the validity of information
19 provided by the vendor. The committee is also strongly encouraged
20 to evaluate available materials not submitted to the department by
21 a vendor.
22 (b) Critically evaluate literacy professional development. The
23 committee shall evaluate with research-based outcomes, using the
24 committee's expertise in the field of literacy. The committee shall
25 assign grade tiers to the professional development based on the
26 professional development's likelihood to increase student literacy
27 outcomes. Priority must be given to professional development that
28 already has a demonstrated history of increasing student outcomes.
29 The committee may perform an evaluation under this subdivision
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

260
1 based on submissions from a vendor, but must independently verify
2 the validity of information provided by the vendor. The committee
3 is also strongly encouraged to evaluate available materials not
4 submitted to the department by a vendor.
5 (c) Critically evaluate other applicable literacy tools or
6 services the committee determines to have a high likelihood or
7 demonstrated history of increasing student literacy outcomes. The
8 committee may perform an evaluation under this subdivision based on
9 submissions from a vendor, but must independently verify the
10 validity of information provided by the vendor. The committee is
11 also strongly encouraged to evaluate available materials not
12 submitted to the department by a vendor.
13 (d) Create, maintain, and post annually on a publicly
14 available website a rankings list of all early literacy series and
15 other items the committee has evaluated. This includes items from
16 each part of the rankings list, items evaluated in the 2024-2025
17 school year, and any subsequent items evaluated going forward. The
18 committee shall denote on the list whether major changes have been
19 made to an early literacy series or other item on the list since
20 the early literacy series or other item was evaluated by the
21 committee and if the early literacy series or other item has not
22 been evaluated since the last ranking. The rankings list created in
23 this subdivision must align with the list of evidence-based tier 1,
24 classwide elementary reading curricula and materials that are
25 aligned with science of reading methods that research has shown to
26 improve literacy outcomes and help pupils achieve reading
27 proficiency as required under section 1280f of the revised school

2 Sec. 35p. (1) From the state school aid fund money
3 appropriated in section 11, there is allocated for 2026-2027 only
4 an amount not to exceed $50,000,000.00 to implement high-impact
5 tutoring programs through eligible tutoring partners in eligible
6 districts.
7 (2) Eligible tutoring partners shall apply for funding in a
8 form and manner determined by the department. An application for
9 funding described in this subsection must include at least all of
10 the following:
11 (a) An identification of a district or intermediate district
12 that will serve as a fiscal agent for awarded funds.
13 (b) A list of district partners that will be recipients of
14 high-impact tutoring from the eligible tutoring partner, including
15 a letter of support from each district that affirms their
16 commitment to ensure alignment with tier 1 instruction,
17 implementation fidelity, including, but not limited to, student
18 usage and frequency, and participation in data sharing and training
19 necessary to maximize outcomes.
20 (c) Projections for the number of students who will be served,
21 broken down by partner district.
22 (d) A description of the efficacious nature of the eligible
23 tutoring partner's program, including the ability of the eligible
24 tutoring partner to hit certain benchmarks and goals, based on
25 evidence.
26 (e) A plan for how the eligible tutoring partner will target
27 high-impact tutoring for the highest need student populations based
28 on some or all of the following criteria:
29 (i) Statewide assessment data.
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

269
1 (ii) Screeners or formative assessment scores.
2 (iii) Economically disadvantaged status.
3 (iv) Special education status.
4 (v) English language learner status.
5 (f) A planned budget for the high-impact tutoring program
6 provided, including the total dollars necessary to serve the
7 identified student population and an estimate of the percentage of
8 awarded dollars that would be directly deployed for high-impact
9 tutoring.
10 (g) A commitment to provide periodic reporting to each
11 district served and to the department, in a form and manner
12 determined by the department, to ensure funds are being spent in
13 accordance with this section.
14 (h) A commitment to return any or all funds awarded if the
15 department determines dollars are used in a way that violates this
16 section.
17 (i) Any additional information required by the department to
18 make informed decisions on awarding competitive grants under this
19 section and to ensure financial stewardship awarded dollars.
20 (3) The department shall award grants to applicants on a
21 competitive basis, using at least all of the following in making
22 award determinations:
23 (a) The cost effectiveness of the application based on the
24 number of students projected to be served, the total requested
25 budget, and the demonstrated outcome capacity of the high-impact
26 tutoring program.
27 (b) The reasonability that the eligible tutoring partner will
28 be able to meet the stated outcome benchmarks.
29 (c) Other criteria, as determined by the department, to ensure
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

270
1 grants are awarded to the most impactful applicants.
2 (4) If the amount allocated in subsection (1) is not
3 sufficient to fully fund all eligible tutoring partners that apply
4 for funding, the department must prioritize grant awards to
5 districts with the lowest levels of literacy proficiency, as
6 determined by student scores on the Michigan student test of
7 educational progress (M-STEP).
8 (5) The department may approve applications from eligible
9 tutoring partners that offer innovative ways to reach students at a
10 lower cost by waiving elements of high-impact tutoring described in
11 subsection (11). This must include, but is not limited to, offering
12 virtual programs. In such cases, the eligible tutoring partner
13 submitting the application must provide evidence of program impact
14 and implement an outcomes-based contract model.
15 (6) It is the intent of the legislature that the work
16 associated with the grant application, oversight, and
17 administration of awards under this section be performed primarily
18 by the eligible tutoring partner and not by districts that have
19 agreed to receive high-impact tutoring.
20 (7) If a district or intermediate district agrees to receive
21 high-impact tutoring from an eligible tutoring partner, the

23 (a) Work with the eligible tutoring partner to identify
24 students that would benefit from the program. The district or
25 intermediate district must communicate with parents or legal
26 guardians of identified students to describe the available program,
27 outline possible student outcomes, and encourage student
28 participation.
29 (b) To the extent possible, integrate the high-impact tutoring
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

271
1 program with the district's Michigan Integrated Continuous
2 Improvement Process (MICIP) plan.

5 provided in this subdivision, forward the amount awarded to the
6 tutoring partner. The designated fiscal agent may retain up to 0.5%
7 of the funding awarded to the tutoring partner for administrative
8 purposes.

17 (11) As used in this section:
18 (a) "Eligible tutoring partner" means a tutoring provider,

22 provides high-impact tutoring and meets at least 1 of the

26 University.

CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

273
1 (iii) Is a vetted high-impact tutoring program included in the
2 MiStrategyBank. If necessary, the Michigan Association of
3 Intermediate School Administrators may convene a committee to
4 review tutoring programs for inclusion in the MiStrategyBank.
5 (b) "High-impact tutoring" means, except as otherwise provided
6 in this section, in-person tutoring that meets all of the
7 following:
8 (i) Occurs during the school day.
9 (ii) Is individualized, with tutor ratios not higher than 1:4.
10 (iii) Provides intensive support with a minimum of 2 sessions
11 per week.
12 (iv) Uses a structured, evidence-based curriculum designed for
13 tutoring, with formative assessments to track progress.
14 (v) Uses trained tutors.

12 following the determination.
13 (4) State funds are allocated on a total approved cost basis.
14 Federal funds are allocated under applicable federal requirements.
15 (5) From the amount allocated in subsection (1), there is

18 necessary costs incurred by a district or intermediate district in

20 education that became effective on July 1, 1987. As used in this
21 subsection, "net increase in necessary costs" means the necessary
22 additional costs incurred solely because of new or revised
23 requirements in the administrative rules minus cost savings
24 permitted in implementing the revised rules. The department shall
25 determine net increase in necessary costs in a manner specified by
26 the department.

1 apprenticeship, or apprentice readiness program.

23 college courses offered at the high school and taught by college

25 instruction.

10 careers and postsecondary education with special attention given to
11 groups of pupils who are at-risk and underrepresented in technical
12 professions and careers.
13 Sec. 67. (1) From the general fund money appropriated in
14 section 11, there is allocated an amount not to exceed
15 $3,000,000.00 for 2025-2026 2026-2027 for college access programs.
16 The programs funded under this section are intended to inform
17 students of college and career options, to provide resources
18 intended to increase the number of pupils who are adequately
19 prepared with the information needed to make informed decisions on
20 college and career, support adult learners, support college
21 completion, and support workforce and employer engagement. The
22 funds appropriated under this section are intended to be used to
23 increase the number of Michigan residents with high-quality degrees
24 or credentials. Funds appropriated under this section must not be
25 used to supplant funding for counselors already funded by
26 districts.
27 (2) The department of lifelong education, advancement, and
28 potential shall administer funds allocated under this section in
29 collaboration with the Michigan college access network. These funds
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

320
1 may be used for any of the following purposes:
2 (a) Michigan college access network operations, programming,
3 and services to local college access networks.
4 (b) Local college access networks, which are community-based
5 college access/success partnerships committed to increasing the
6 college participation and completion rates within geographically
7 defined communities through a coordinated strategy.
8 (c) The Michigan college advising program, a program intended
9 to place trained, recently graduated college advisors in high
10 schools that serve significant numbers of low-income and first-
11 generation college-going pupils. State funds used for this purpose
12 may not exceed 33% of the total funds available under this
13 subsection.
14 (d) Subgrants of up to $5,000.00 to districts with
15 comprehensive high schools that establish a college access team and
16 implement specific strategies to create a college-going culture in
17 a high school in a form and manner approved by the Michigan college
18 access network and the department of lifelong education,
19 advancement, and potential.
20 (e) The Michigan college access portal, an online one-stop
21 portal to help pupils and families plan and apply for college.
22 (f) Public awareness and outreach campaigns to encourage low-
23 income and first-generation college-going pupils to take necessary
24 steps toward college and to assist pupils and families in
25 completing a timely and accurate free application for federal
26 student aid.
27 (g) Subgrants to postsecondary institutions to recruit, hire,
28 and train college student mentors and college advisors to assist
29 high school pupils in navigating the postsecondary planning and
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

1 enrollment process.
2 (3) For the purposes of this section, "college" means any
3 postsecondary educational opportunity that leads to a career,
4 including, but not limited to, a postsecondary degree, industry-
5 recognized technical certification, or registered apprenticeship.

1 (3) A district shall exempt a student from the requirement to
2 complete the FAFSA if any of the following are met:
3 (a) The student's parent or legal guardian, or the student if
4 the student is 18 years of age or older, is an emancipated minor,
5 or is an unaccompanied youth, has submitted a parental waiver to
6 the district exempting the student from completing the FAFSA. The
7 parental waiver described in this subdivision must be obtained
8 through a standard form developed by the department of lifelong
9 education, advancement, and potential.
10 (b) The student is unable to complete the FAFSA because of
11 privacy concerns.
12 (c) All of the following are met:
13 (i) After a good-faith effort, the student's parent or legal
14 guardian refuses to sign the parental waiver, is unresponsive, or
15 cannot sign the parental waiver.
16 (ii) The student is unable to complete the FAFSA as an
17 independent student.
18 (iii) The student agrees to opt out of completing the FAFSA.
19 (iv) Other than the requirements in subsection (2), the student
20 is on track to graduate.
21 (v) A school administrator of the student's high school
22 demonstrates to the board that good-faith efforts have been made to
23 assist the student or the student's parent or legal guardian in
24 completing the FAFSA or obtaining a parental waiver.
25 (vi) The board ensures compliance with 42 USC 11432(g)(6)(A).

23 Sec. 94a. (1) There is created within the state budget office

29 postsecondary institutions.
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

329
1 (b) Create, maintain, and enhance this state's P-20
2 longitudinal data system and ensure that it meets the requirements
3 of subsection (4).

5 reduce the administrative burden on reporting entities, including,
6 but not limited to, electronic transcript services.
7 (d) Create, maintain, and enhance this state's web-based
8 educational portal to provide information to school leaders,
9 teachers, researchers, and the public in compliance with all

11 limited to, all of the following:
12 (i) Data sets that link teachers to student information,
13 allowing districts to assess individual teacher impact on student
14 performance and consider student growth factors in teacher and
15 principal evaluation systems.

17 regional data hubs that, in combination with local data, can
18 improve teaching and learning in the classroom.
19 (iii) Research-ready data sets for researchers to perform
20 research that advances this state's educational performance.
21 (e) Provide data in a useful manner to allow state and local
22 policymakers to make informed policy decisions.
23 (f) Provide public reports to the residents of this state to
24 allow them to assess allocation of resources and the return on
25 their investment in the education system of this state.

29 postsecondary institutions as required under state or federal law

1 achievement.

7 and postsecondary, meets all of the following:
8 (i) Contains a unique statewide student identifier that does
9 not permit a student to be individually identified by users of the
10 system, except as allowed by federal and state law.

24 shared education interests and outcomes.

26 which students exit, transfer in, transfer out, drop out, or
27 complete education programs.

29 systems.

1 Research Institute shall submit a report to the house and senate

1 (iii) Promote and distribute the most current instructional

12 provide mentors with research-based training and technical
13 assistance designed to help more pupils be successful virtual
14 learners.
15 (vii) Convene focus groups and conduct annual surveys of
16 teachers, administrators, pupils, parents, and others to identify
17 barriers and opportunities related to virtual learning.
18 (viii) Produce an annual consumer awareness report for schools
19 and parents about effective virtual education providers and
20 education delivery models, performance data, cost structures, and
21 research trends.
22 (ix) Provide an internet-based platform that educators can use
23 to create student-centric learning tools and resources for sharing
24 in the state's open educational resource repository and facilitate
25 a user network that assists educators in using the content creation
26 platform and state repository for open educational resources. As
27 part of this initiative, the Michigan Virtual University shall work
28 collaboratively with districts and intermediate districts to
29 establish a plan to make available virtual resources that align to
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

339
1 Michigan's K to 12 curriculum standards for use by students,
2 educators, and parents.
3 (x) Create and maintain a public statewide catalog of virtual
4 learning courses being offered by all public schools and community
5 colleges in this state. The Michigan Virtual Learning Research
6 Institute shall identify and develop a list of nationally
7 recognized best practices for virtual learning and use this list to
8 support reviews of virtual course vendors, courses, and
9 instructional practices. The Michigan Virtual Learning Research
10 Institute shall also provide a mechanism for intermediate districts
11 to use the identified best practices to review content offered by
12 constituent districts. The Michigan Virtual Learning Research
13 Institute shall review the virtual course offerings of the Michigan
14 Virtual University, and make the results from these reviews
15 available to the public as part of the statewide catalog. The
16 Michigan Virtual Learning Research Institute shall ensure that the
17 statewide catalog is made available to the public on the Michigan
18 Virtual University website and shall allow the ability to link it
19 to each district's website as provided for in section 21f. The
20 statewide catalog must also contain all of the following:
21 (A) The number of enrollments in each virtual course in the
22 immediately preceding school year.
23 (B) The number of enrollments that earned 60% or more of the
24 total course points for each virtual course in the immediately
25 preceding school year.
26 (C) The pass rate for each virtual course.
27 (xi) Support registration, payment services, and transcript
28 functionality for the statewide catalog and train key stakeholders
29 on how to use new features.
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

340
1 (xii) Collaborate with key stakeholders to examine district
2 level accountability and teacher effectiveness issues related to
3 virtual learning under section 21f and make findings and
4 recommendations publicly available.
5 (xiii) Provide a report on the activities of the Michigan
6 Virtual Learning Research Institute.
7 (3) To further enhance its expertise and leadership in virtual
8 learning, the Michigan Virtual University shall continue to operate
9 the Michigan Virtual School as a statewide laboratory and quality
10 model of instruction by implementing virtual and blended learning
11 solutions for Michigan schools in accordance with the following
12 parameters:
13 (a) The Michigan Virtual School must maintain its
14 accreditation status from recognized national and international
15 accrediting entities.
16 (b) The Michigan Virtual University shall use no more than
17 $1,000,000.00 of the amount allocated under this section to
18 subsidize the cost paid by districts for virtual courses.
19 (c) In providing educators responsible for the teaching of
20 virtual courses as provided for in this section, the Michigan
21 Virtual School shall follow the requirements to request and assess,
22 and the department of state police shall provide, a criminal
23 history check and criminal records check under sections 1230 and
24 1230a of the revised school code, MCL 380.1230 and 380.1230a, in
25 the same manner as if the Michigan Virtual School were a school
26 district under those sections.
27 (4) From the funds allocated under subsection (1), the
28 Michigan Virtual University shall support the expansion of new
29 online and blended educator professional development programs.

17 (12) As used in this section:
18 (a) "Blended learning" means a hybrid instructional delivery
19 model where pupils are provided content, instruction, and
20 assessment, in part at a supervised educational facility away from
21 home where the pupil and a teacher with a valid Michigan teaching
22 certificate are in the same physical location and in part through
23 internet-connected learning environments with some degree of pupil
24 control over time, location, and pace of instruction.
25 (b) "Cyber school" means a full-time instructional program of
26 virtual courses for pupils that may or may not require attendance
27 at a physical school location.
28 (c) "Virtual course" means a course of study that is capable
29 of generating a credit or a grade and that is provided in an
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

344
1 interactive learning environment in which the majority of the
2 curriculum is delivered using the internet and in which pupils are
3 separated from their instructor or teacher of record by time or
4 location, or both.
5 Sec. 98d. (1) From the state school aid fund money

14 state's pre-K to 12 educational standards over the air, online, and
15 in communities.

17 each day for 7 days each week of quality instructional content that
18 is aligned with this state's pre-K to 12 educational standards.
19 Over-the-air broadcasts as described in this subdivision must be
20 streamed live and must be archived for on-demand viewing on a
21 companion website, along with additional learning materials
22 relevant to lessons.
23 (c) The MLC must be managed and operated by DPTV, and DPTV
24 shall assume all risk, liability, and responsibility for the MLC in
25 accordance with regulations by the United States Federal
26 Communications Commission, PBS broadcast standards, and standard
27 nonprofit business standards. DPTV shall serve as the fiduciary
28 agent and service manager for the MLC. The MLC shall originate from
29 a central operations center that is responsible for providing the
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

345
1 infrastructure, content, and engagement of the MLC in partnership
2 with this state's educational leadership organizations.

4 funding, staff training, and central management of the MLC to
5 station partners to insert additional channels into each station's
6 broadcast streams and to support staffing and engagement as
7 outlined in a memorandum of understanding among the stations.

9 other Michigan public television stations, including, but not
10 limited to, WKAR, WGVU, WDCQ, WCMU, and WNMU, to deliver the over-
11 the-air MLC broadcasts described in this section and to support

28 and intermediate districts for school-based driver's training

CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

357
1 (2) Grants under this section must be used to pay for the
2 costs of implementing a driver's training program. Costs may
3 include, but are not limited to, salaries and benefits for staff,
4 vehicle purchases or leases, insurance, materials and equipment,
5 and any other costs necessary to run a program.
6 (3) To receive funding under this section, districts and
7 intermediate districts must apply in a form and manner prescribed
8 by the department. An applicant shall certify that the applicant
9 will run the program using district or intermediate district
10 employees and will not outsource any programming to an outside
11 third party.
12 (4) The maximum grant amount for an applicant is $500,000.00.
13 The department shall prioritize applicants that received a grant
14 for the same purpose in a previous year, including awards under
15 this section, former section 99ii, and former section 99jj, as long
16 as the applicant continues the driver's training program for
17 another year. Afterwards, the department shall prioritize awards on
18 a first-come, first-served basis. No district or intermediate
19 district may receive more than 1 grant in a single fiscal year.
20 (5) The funds allocated under this section for 2026-2027 are a
21 work project appropriation, and any unexpended funds for 2026-2027
22 are carried forward into 2027-2028. The purpose of the work project
23 is to continue support for district- and intermediate-district-run
24 driver's training programs. The estimated completion date of the
25 work project is September 30, 2031.

27 payments under this section on a schedule determined by the

29 Sec. 99s. (1) From state school aid fund money appropriated
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

358
1 under section 11, there is allocated for 2026-2027 an amount not to
2 exceed $8,000,000.00 for Michigan science, technology, engineering,
3 and mathematics programs. Programs funded under this section are
4 intended to increase the number of pupils demonstrating proficiency
5 in science and mathematics on the state assessments, to increase
6 the number of pupils who are college- and career-ready upon high
7 school graduation, and to promote certificate and degree attainment
8 in STEM fields. Notwithstanding section 17b, the department shall
9 make payments under this section on a schedule determined by the
10 department.
11 (2) The MiSTEM council annually shall review and make
12 recommendations to the governor, the legislature, and the
13 department concerning changes to the statewide strategy adopted by
14 the council for delivering STEM education-related opportunities to
15 pupils. The MiSTEM council shall use funds received under this
16 section to ensure that the MiSTEM council's members or their
17 designees are trained in a third-party evaluation system such as
18 the Change the Equation STEMworks program for the purpose of rating
19 STEM programs.
20 (3) The MiSTEM council shall make specific funding
21 recommendations for the funds allocated under subsection (4) by
22 December 15 of each fiscal year. Each specific funding
23 recommendation must be for a program or demonstration grant
24 approved by the MiSTEM council. All of the following apply:
25 (a) To be eligible for MiSTEM council approval as described in
26 this subsection, a program must satisfy all of the following:
27 (i) Align with this state's academic standards.
28 (ii) Have STEMworks or other third-party certification.
29 (iii) Provide project-based experiential learning, student
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

359
1 programming, or educator professional learning experiences.
2 (iv) Focus predominantly on classroom-based STEM experiences or
3 professional learning experiences.
4 (b) The MiSTEM council shall approve programs that represent
5 all network regions and include a diverse array of options for
6 students and educators.
7 (c) To be eligible for MiSTEM council approval as described in
8 this subsection, a demonstration grant must satisfy all of the
9 following:
10 (i) Align with this state's academic standards.
11 (ii) In collaboration with the department, scale project,
12 problem, or place-based learning in a building or district in
13 support of the statewide STEM strategy as recommended by the MiSTEM
14 council.
15 (d) The MiSTEM council is encouraged to work with the MiSTEM
16 network to develop locally and regionally developed programs,
17 demonstration grants, and professional learning experiences for the
18 programs on the list of approved programs. At the direction of the
19 MiSTEM council, funds can be used to evaluate and market regions,
20 programs, and demonstration grants.
21 (e) If the MiSTEM council is unable to make specific funding
22 recommendations by December 15 of a fiscal year, the department
23 shall award and distribute the funds allocated under subsection (4)
24 on a competitive grant basis that at least follows the statewide
25 STEM strategy plan and rating system recommended by the MiSTEM
26 council. Each grant must provide STEM education-related
27 opportunities for pupils.

CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

360
1 exceed $3,800,000.00 for the purpose of funding programs or

12 to 12 pupils enrolled in districts within each region in the
13 immediately preceding fiscal year.
14 (6) A MiSTEM network region shall do all of the following:
15 (a) Collaborate with the career and educational advisory
16 council that is located in the MiSTEM region and the department to
17 develop a regional strategic plan for STEM education that creates a
18 robust regional STEM culture, that empowers STEM teachers, that
19 integrates business and education into the STEM network, and that
20 ensures high-quality STEM experiences for pupils. At a minimum, a
21 regional STEM strategic plan described in this subdivision should
22 do all of the following in collaboration with the department:
23 (i) Identify regional employer need for STEM.
24 (ii) Identify processes for regional employers and educators to
25 create guided pathways for STEM careers that include internships or
26 externships, apprenticeships, and other experiential engagements
27 for pupils.
28 (iii) Identify educator professional learning opportunities,
29 including internships or externships and apprenticeships, that
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

361
1 integrate this state's science standards into high-quality STEM
2 experiences that engage pupils.
3 (b) Facilitate regional STEM events such as educator and
4 employer networking and STEM career fairs to raise STEM awareness.
5 (c) Contribute to the MiSTEM website and engage in other
6 MiSTEM network functions to further the mission of STEM in this
7 state in coordination with the MiSTEM council and the department of
8 labor and economic opportunity.
9 (d) Facilitate application and implementation of state and
10 federal funds under this subsection and any other grants or funds
11 for the MiSTEM network region.
12 (e) Work with districts to provide STEM programming and
13 professional learning.
14 (f) Coordinate recurring discussions and work with the career
15 and educational advisory council to ensure that feedback and best
16 practices are being shared, including funding, program,
17 professional learning opportunities, and regional strategic plans.
18 (g) In collaboration with the department, deploy marketing and
19 outreach efforts to publish the availability of STEM learning
20 opportunities.
21 (7) To receive funds under this section, a grant recipient
22 must allow access for the department or the department's designee
23 to audit all records related to the program for which the grant
24 recipient received those funds. The grant recipient shall reimburse
25 the state for all disallowances found in the audit.
26 (8) To receive funds under this section, a grant recipient
27 must provide at least a 10% local match from local public or
28 private resources for the funds received under this subsection.

CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

2 Sec. 101. (1) To be eligible to receive state aid under this
3 article, not later than the fifth Wednesday after the pupil
4 membership count day and not later than the fifth Wednesday after
5 the supplemental count day, each district superintendent shall
6 submit and certify to the center and the intermediate
7 superintendent, in the form and manner prescribed by the center,
8 the number of pupils enrolled and in regular daily attendance,
9 including identification of tuition-paying pupils, in the district
10 as of the pupil membership count day and as of the supplemental
11 count day, as applicable, for the current school year. In addition,
12 a district maintaining school during the entire year shall submit
13 and certify to the center and the intermediate superintendent, in
14 the form and manner prescribed by the center, the number of pupils
15 enrolled and in regular daily attendance in the district for the
16 current school year pursuant to rules promulgated by the
17 superintendent. Not later than the sixth Wednesday after the pupil
18 membership count day and not later than the sixth Wednesday after
19 the supplemental count day, the district shall resolve any pupil
20 membership conflicts with another district, correct any data
21 issues, and recertify the data in a form and manner prescribed by
22 the center and file the certified data with the intermediate
23 superintendent. If a district fails to submit and certify the
24 attendance data, as required under this subsection, the center
25 shall notify the department and the department shall withhold state
26 aid due to be distributed under this article from the defaulting
27 district immediately, beginning with the next payment after the
28 failure and continuing with each payment until the district
29 complies with this subsection. If a district does not comply with
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

364
1 this subsection by the end of the fiscal year, the district
2 forfeits the amount withheld. A person who willfully falsifies a
3 figure or statement in the certified and sworn copy of enrollment
4 is subject to penalty as prescribed by section 161.
5 (2) To be eligible to receive state aid under this article,
6 not later than the twenty-fourth Wednesday after the pupil
7 membership count day and not later than the twenty-fourth Wednesday
8 after the supplemental count day, an intermediate district shall
9 submit to the center, in a form and manner prescribed by the

17 with each payment until the intermediate district complies with
18 this subsection. If an intermediate district does not comply with
19 this subsection by the end of the fiscal year, the intermediate
20 district forfeits the amount withheld.

1 collective bargaining agreement. A district may apply for a waiver

4 (b) Except as otherwise provided in this article, a district
5 failing to comply with the required minimum hours and days of pupil
6 instruction under this subsection forfeits from its total state aid
7 allocation an amount determined by applying a ratio of the number
8 of hours or days the district was in noncompliance in relation to
9 the required minimum number of hours and days under this
10 subsection. Not later than the first business day in August, the
11 board of each district shall either certify to the department that
12 the district was in full compliance with this section regarding the
13 number of hours and days of pupil instruction in the previous
14 school year, or report to the department, in a form and manner
15 prescribed by the center, each instance of noncompliance. If the
16 district did not provide at least the required minimum number of
17 hours and days of pupil instruction under this subsection, the
18 department shall make the deduction of state aid in the following
19 fiscal year from the first payment of state school aid. A district
20 is not subject to forfeiture of funds under this subsection for a
21 fiscal year in which a forfeiture was already imposed under
22 subsection (6).

24 conferences are not counted as hours or days of pupil instruction.

26 this subsection, if a district does not have at least 75% of the
27 district's membership in attendance on any day of pupil
28 instruction, the department shall pay the district state aid in
29 that proportion of 1/180 that the actual percent of attendance
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

366
1 bears to 75%.
2 (e) If a district adds 1 or more days of pupil instruction to
3 the end of its instructional calendar for a school year to comply
4 with subdivision (a) because the district otherwise would fail to
5 provide the required minimum number of days of pupil instruction
6 even after the operation of subsection (4) due to conditions not
7 within the control of school authorities, then subdivision (d) does
8 not apply for any day of pupil instruction that is added to the end
9 of the instructional calendar. Instead, for any of those days, if
10 the district does not have at least 60% of the district's
11 membership in attendance on that day, the department shall pay the
12 district state aid in that proportion of 1/180 that the actual
13 percentage of attendance bears to 60%. For any day of pupil
14 instruction added to the instructional calendar as described in

17 the form and manner prescribed by the department.

11 (4) All of the following apply to the counting of days and
12 hours of pupil instruction under this section:

14 6 days or the equivalent number of hours for which pupil

16 control of school authorities, such as severe storms, fires,
17 epidemics, utility power unavailability, water or sewer failure, or
18 health conditions as defined by the city, county, or state health
19 authorities, are counted as hours and days of pupil instruction.

21 instruction, the department shall count as hours and days of pupil
22 instruction for a fiscal year not more than 3 additional days or
23 the equivalent number of additional hours for which pupil
24 instruction is not provided in a district due to unusual and
25 extenuating occurrences resulting from conditions not within the
26 control of school authorities such as those conditions described in

8 for a district, the superintendent of public instruction shall not
