5 (10) A district may count up to 38 hours of professional
6 development for teachers as hours of pupil instruction. All of the
7 following apply to the counting of professional development as
8 pupil instruction under this subsection:
9 (a) If the professional development exceeds 5 hours in a
10 single day, that day may be counted as a day of pupil instruction.
11 (b) At least 8 hours of the professional development counted
12 as hours of pupil instruction under this subsection must be
13 recommended by a districtwide professional development advisory
14 committee appointed by the district board. The advisory committee
15 must be composed of teachers employed by the district who represent
16 a variety of grades and subject matter specializations, including
17 special education; nonteaching staff; parents; and administrators.
18 The majority membership of the committee must be composed of
19 teaching staff.
20 (c) Professional development provided online is allowable and
21 encouraged, as long as the instruction has been approved by the
22 district. The department shall issue a list of approved online
23 professional development providers that must include the Michigan
24 Virtual School.
25 (d) Professional development may only be counted as hours of
26 pupil instruction under this subsection for the pupils of those
27 teachers scheduled to participate in the professional development.
28 (e) The professional development must meet all of the
29 following to be counted as pupil instruction under this subsection:
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

375
1 (i) Be aligned to the school or district improvement plan for
2 the school or district in which the professional development is
3 being provided.
4 (ii) Be linked to 1 or more criteria in the evaluation tool
5 developed or adopted by the district or intermediate district under
6 section 1249 of the revised school code, MCL 380.1249.
7 (iii) Has been approved by the department as counting for state
8 continuing education clock hours. The number of hours of
9 professional development counted as hours of pupil instruction
10 under this subsection may not exceed the number of state continuing
11 education clock hours for which the professional development was
12 approved.
13 (iv) Not more than a combined total of 10 hours of the
14 professional development takes place before the first scheduled day
15 of school for the school year ending in the fiscal year and after
16 the last scheduled day of school for that school year.
17 (v) Not more than 10 hours of the professional development
18 takes place in a single month.
19 (vi) At least 75% of teachers scheduled to participate in the
20 professional development are in attendance.
21 (11) Subsections (3) and (8) do not apply to a school of
22 excellence that is a cyber school, as that term is defined in
23 section 551 of the revised school code, MCL 380.551, and is in
24 compliance with section 553a of the revised school code, MCL
25 380.553a. Beginning July 1, 2021, this subsection is subject to

9 (19) As used in this section:
10 (a) "Career and educational advisory council" means an
11 advisory council to the local workforce development boards located
12 in a prosperity region consisting of educational, employer, labor,
13 and parent representatives.
14 (b) "Career pathway" means a combination of rigorous and high-
15 quality education, training, and other services that comply with
16 all of the following:
17 (i) Aligns with the skill needs of industries in the economy of
18 this state or in the regional economy involved.
19 (ii) Prepares an individual to be successful in any of a full
20 range of secondary or postsecondary education options, including
21 apprenticeships registered under the act of August 16, 1937,
22 commonly referred to as the national apprenticeship act, 29 USC 50
23 et seq.
24 (iii) Includes counseling to support an individual in achieving
25 the individual's education and career goals.
26 (iv) Includes, as appropriate, education offered concurrently
27 with and in the same context as workforce preparation activities
28 and training for a specific occupation or occupational cluster.
29 (v) Organizes education, training, and other services to meet
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

393
1 the particular needs of an individual in a manner that accelerates
2 the educational and career advancement of the individual to the
3 extent practicable.
4 (vi) Enables an individual to attain a secondary school diploma
5 or its recognized equivalent, and at least 1 recognized
6 postsecondary credential.
7 (vii) Helps an individual enter or advance within a specific
8 occupation or occupational cluster.
9 (c) "Department" means the department of labor and economic
10 opportunity.

15 process described in subsection (5)(c) and approved by the

8 Sec. 147b. (1) The MPSERS retirement obligation reform reserve
9 fund is created as a separate account within the state school aid
10 fund.
11 (2) The state treasurer may receive money or other assets from
12 any source for deposit into the MPSERS retirement obligation reform
13 reserve fund. The state treasurer shall direct the investment of
14 the MPSERS retirement obligation reform reserve fund. The state
15 treasurer shall credit to the MPSERS retirement obligation reform
16 reserve fund interest and earnings from the MPSERS retirement
17 obligation reform reserve fund.
18 (3) Money available in the MPSERS retirement obligation reform
19 reserve fund must not be expended without a specific appropriation.
20 (4) Money in the MPSERS retirement obligation reform reserve
21 fund at the close of the fiscal year remains in the MPSERS
22 retirement obligation reform reserve fund and does not lapse lapses
23 to the state school aid fund or to the general fund. The department
24 of treasury is the administrator of the MPSERS retirement
25 obligation reform reserve fund for auditing purposes.
26 (5) For 2022-2023, $825,000,000.00 from the state school aid
27 fund is deposited into the MPSERS retirement obligation reform
28 reserve fund. It is the intent of the legislature that
29 $425,000,000.00 of the funds deposited under this subsection for

1 administrative rule for the requirement. The form must be posted on

6 administrative rule of this state during each applicable school

8 the department. This section does not require a nonpublic school to
9 submit a form described in subsection (2). A nonpublic school is
10 not eligible for reimbursement under this section if the nonpublic

12 timely manner.

16 manner. The superintendent shall determine the amount of funds to

18 the nonpublic school's actual costs in complying with a health,
19 safety, or welfare requirement under a law or administrative rule
20 of this state. The superintendent shall calculate a nonpublic
21 school's actual cost in accordance with this section.
22 (5) If the funds allocated under this section are insufficient
23 to fully fund payments as otherwise calculated under this section,
24 the department shall distribute funds under this section on a
25 prorated or other equitable basis as determined by the
26 superintendent.

1 this section. If a nonpublic school does not allow the department

4 (7) The funds appropriated under this section are for purposes
5 that are incidental to teaching and the provision of educational
6 services to nonpublic school students; that are noninstructional in
7 nature; that do not constitute a primary function or element
8 necessary for a nonpublic school's existence, operation, and
9 survival; that do not involve or result in excessive religious
10 entanglement; and that are intended for the public purpose of

13 described in this section.

24 requirement under a law or administrative rule of this state
25 identified by the department under subsection (2) and is to be

28 costs. The nonpublic school shall not charge more than the hourly
29 wage of its lowest-paid employee capable of performing a specific
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

407
1 task regardless of whether that individual is available and
2 regardless of who actually performs a specific task. Labor costs
3 under this subsection must be estimated and charged in increments
4 of 15 minutes or more, with all partial time increments rounded
5 down. When calculating costs under subsection (4), fee components
6 must be itemized in a manner that expresses both the hourly wage
7 and the number of hours charged. The nonpublic school may not
8 charge any applicable labor charge amount to cover or partially
9 cover the cost of health or fringe benefits. A nonpublic school
10 shall not charge any overtime wages in the calculation of labor
11 costs.

6 disaster, death or serious illness of the individual or another
7 employee, an emergency school closure, fraud or other intentional
8 wrongdoing of the individual or another employee, or an emergency
9 health condition as defined by city, county, or state health
10 authorities.
11 (5) There must be no deduction under subsection (2) for
12 employment of an individual if there is a membership adjustment
13 under section 15 based on the same employment.This section provides
14 the sole and exclusive penalty for a violation of subsection (1). A
15 district or intermediate district must not also be subject to a
16 membership adjustment under section 15 for a violation of
17 subsection (1).
18 (6) If a school official is notified by the department that
19 the school official is employing an individual in violation of this
20 section and knowingly continues to employ that individual for a
21 period longer than 10 school days, the school official is guilty of
22 a misdemeanor punishable by a fine of $1,500.00 for each incident.
23 This penalty is in addition to all other financial penalties
24 otherwise specified in this article.
25 Sec. 164l. (1) Not later than 30 days after the enactment of
26 the any amendatory act that added to this section, article, the
27 legislature house and senate shall provide to the responsible
28 entity and the state budget director a list of legislatively
29 directed spending items, which may be referred to in this section
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

414
1 as grants or direct appropriation grants, funded under the
2 amendatory act that added this section consistent with house or
3 senate rules and this section. as defined in sections 364 and 364a
4 of the management and budget act, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a, that
5 are funded by the amendatory act. The list must include all
6 information and documents pertaining to the funded items as
7 publicly disclosed in accordance with house or senate rules and
8 this section.under sections 364 and 364a of the management and
9 budget act, MCL 18.1364 and 18.1364a.
10 (2) Notwithstanding any other conditions or requirements for
11 direct appropriation grants, the responsible entity shall perform,
12 at a minimum, at least all of the following activities to
13 administer the grants described in subsection (1):
14 (a) Establish a process to review, complete, and execute a
15 grant agreement with a grant recipient. The responsible entity
16 shall not execute a grant agreement unless all necessary
17 documentation has been submitted and reviewed.
18 (b) Verify to the extent possible that a grant recipient is a
19 not-for-profit entity and will use funds as publicly disclosed and
20 for a public purpose that serves the economic prosperity, health,
21 safety, or general welfare of the residents of this state.
22 (c) Review and verify all necessary information to ensure the
23 grant recipient is reasonably able to execute the grant agreement,
24 perform its fiduciary duty, and comply with all applicable state
25 and federal statutes. The responsible entity may deduct the cost of
26 background checks and any other efforts performed as part of this
27 verification from the amount of the designated grant award.
28 (d) Disburse the grant money per the grant disbursement
29 schedule in the executed grant agreement on a reimbursement basis
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

415
1 after the grantee has provided sufficient documentation, as
2 determined by the responsible entity, to verify that expenditures
3 were made in accordance with the project purpose.
4 (e) If the state budget director determines that information
5 provided by the grantee does not meet the disclosure requirements,
6 that the grant will be used to pay a tax lien, delinquent tax, or
7 other obligation owed to this state, or that the grant will create
8 a conflict of interest, the responsible entity shall not release
9 the grant money to the grantee. Money that is not released under
10 this subdivision lapses at the end of the fiscal year. There is not
11 a conflict of interest if the sponsoring legislator certifies that
12 the sponsoring legislator's immediate family members, legislative
13 staff members that have worked for the sponsoring legislator within
14 the past 2 years, and the sponsoring legislator do not have a
15 direct or indirect pecuniary interest in the legislatively directed
16 spending item.
17 (3) An executed grant agreement under this section between the
18 responsible entity and a grant recipient must include at least all
19 of the following:
20 (a) All necessary identifying information for the grant
21 recipient, including any tax and financial information necessary
22 for the responsible entity to administer grant money under this
23 section.
24 (b) A description of the project for which the grant money
25 will be expended, including tentative timelines and the estimated
26 budget. Project budget must include how all grant money will be
27 used and must indicate if any grant money will be provided to a
28 third party or subrecipient. The responsible entity shall not
29 reimburse expenditures that are outside of the project purpose, as
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

416
1 stated in the executed grant agreement, from appropriations under
2 the amendatory act that added this section. The grantee shall
3 return to the state treasury any interest in excess of $1,000.00
4 earned on the grant money while unexpended and in possession of the
5 grantee.
6 (c) Unless otherwise specified in the responsible entity's
7 policy, a requirement that funds appropriated for the grants
8 described in subsection (1) may be used only for expenditures that
9 occur on or after the effective date of the amendatory act that
10 added this section.
11 (d) A requirement for reporting by the grant recipient to the
12 responsible entity and the legislative sponsor that provides the
13 status of the project and an accounting of all money expended by
14 the grant recipient, as determined by the responsible entity.
15 (e) A clawback provision that allows the department of
16 treasury to recoup or otherwise collect any grant money that is
17 declined, unspent, or otherwise misused.
18 (f) The documents publicly disclosed under subsection (1).
19 (4) If appropriate to improve the administration or oversight
20 of a grant described in subsection (1), the responsible entity may
21 adopt a memorandum of understanding with another state department
22 to perform the required duties under this section.
23 (5) A grant recipient shall respond to all reasonable
24 information requests from the responsible entity related to grant
25 expenditures and retain grant records for not less than 7 years,
26 and the grant may be subject to monitoring, site visits, and audit
27 as determined by the responsible entity. The grant agreement
28 required under this section must include signed assurance by the
29 chief executive officer or other executive officer of the grant
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

417
1 recipient authorized to bind the grant recipient that the
2 requirements of this subsection will be met.
3 (6) The grant recipient shall expend all grant money awarded
4 and complete all projects not later than September 30, 2030. If, at
5 that time, any unexpended money remains, the grant recipient shall
6 return that money to the state treasury. If a grant recipient does
7 not provide information sufficient to execute a grant agreement not
8 later than June 1, 2026, the responsible entity shall return money
9 associated with the grant to the state treasury.
10 (7) Any grant money that is awarded to a responsible entity is
11 appropriated in that responsible entity for the purpose of the
12 intended grant.
13 (8) Except as otherwise provided in subsection (9), beginning
14 March 15 of the current fiscal year, the responsible entity shall
15 post a report in a publicly accessible location on its website. The
16 report must list the grant recipient, project purpose, and location
17 of the project for each grant described in subsection (1), the
18 status of money allocated and disbursed under the grant agreement,
19 and the legislative sponsor, if applicable. The responsible entity
20 shall update the report and post the updated report in a publicly
21 accessible location on its website not later than June 15 of the
22 current fiscal year and again not later than September 15 of the
23 current fiscal year. The responsible entity shall include in the
24 report the most comprehensive information the responsible entity
25 has available at the time of posting for grants awarded.
26 (9) If the state budget office determines that it is more
27 efficient for the state budget office to compile all affected
28 responsible entities' information and post a report of the compiled
29 information rather than the report required under subsection (8)
CRM S06121'26 (S-1) 14suma

418
1 being posted by individual responsible entities, the state budget
2 office may compile that information across all affected responsible
3 entities and other state departments and post the compiled report
4 and any updates on the same time schedule as identified in
5 subsection (8).
6 (10) If the responsible entity reasonably determines that the
7 money allocated for an executed grant agreement under this section
8 was misused or that use of the money was misrepresented by the
9 grant recipient, the responsible entity shall not award any
10 additional funds under the executed grant agreement and shall refer
11 the grant for review following internal audit protocols, which may
12 include referral for criminal investigation.
13 (11) As used in this section, "responsible entity" means the
14 department, the department of lifelong education, advancement, and
15 potential, a district, an intermediate district, or other person
16 that administers a grant under this article.
17 (2) In accordance with section 364 of the management and
18 budget act, 1984 PA 431, MCL 18.1364, the department or agency
19 administering the grant shall post a report in a publicly
20 accessible location on its website beginning March 15 of the
21 current fiscal year. The department or agency shall update the
22 report and shall post an updated report not later than June 15 of
23 the current fiscal year and again not later than September 15 of
24 the current fiscal year. The department shall include in the report
25 the most comprehensive information the department has available at
26 the time of posting for grants awarded.
